The asymptotic claim is correct by construction (the math says so) but not yet benchmarked against a classical GP at realistic sizes. Wall-clock benchmarks at n ∈ {10³, 10⁴, 10⁵} need to be run before treating ABNG as a “GP killer.”

Capability 3 — Tamper-evident training history (audit chain)

Demo: Universal property — every demo asserts it.

What every demo proves

Every state mutation in ABNG funnels through one chain step:

Every demo asserts verify_chain == Ok after training. For an n-event audit log, verify_chain walks the chain forward, recomputing new_hash at every step from the stored previous hash and the stored canonical payload, and compares to the stored value. Any single byte changed in any payload would fail the recomputation at that event AND every subsequent event — a Merkle-style cascade.

Across the 11 demos, this code path runs hundreds of times. The fact that verify_chain == Ok after every demo’s training session is the empirical proof the audit chain is sound under realistic workloads.

The threat model

The audit chain is designed against three concrete attacks:

  1. Post-hoc rewrite. Attacker modifies a historical training observation after the fact. Detection: the modified event’s new_hash no longer matches sha256(previous_hash ‖ payload), AND every subsequent event’s stored previous_hash no longer matches the recomputed-from-modified value. Cascading detection.
  2. Event reordering. Attacker shuffles the order of training events. Detection: each event’s payload includes its seq number; reordering puts events at the wrong seq, breaking the per-event monotonic-seq invariant.
  3. Event substitution. Attacker replaces one event with another that produces the same new_hash. Detection: SHA-256 collision resistance — finding two payloads with the same hash is computationally infeasible.

Data scale: Universal property — every demo’s audit log (3 to ~110 events, depending on workload) gets verified. The largest audit log in the test suite is the PINN demo’s 110-event chain post-asymmetric training. The cryptographic argument scales independently of dataset size: SHA-256 chain verification is O(log(events)) per check and the security guarantees come from the hash function itself, not from the workload. So this is the one capability where “small data demos suffice” is a load-bearing claim, not a hedge.

Code sketch — what an event payload looks like

[seq u64 BE | epoch u64 BE | node_id u32 BE | kind tag u8 |
 kind body (variable) |
 stats_version u64 BE | stats_hash [u8; 32]]

For a BeliefUpdate event (the most common kind, fired on every abng_observe), the kind body is a single value f64-bits BE — 8 bytes. Total payload for a BeliefUpdate is 21 + 8 + 8 + 32 = 69 bytes, hashed into the chain.

The canonical encoding is byte-deterministic: big-endian on every numeric field, fixed sizes, no padding ambiguity. Two ABNG instances run with the same seed and observation order produce identical event payload bytes — and therefore identical chain hashes.

Connection to existing techniques

Technique Hash-chain ML-aware Per-prediction lineage
Git Yes (DAG of commits) No No
MLflow No (timestamped log) Yes Manual
sigstore / in-toto Yes (artifact attestation) No Per-artifact
Blockchain ML provenance Yes (full chain on-chain) Yes Yes, but expensive
ABNG audit chain Yes (linear) Yes (every state mutation) Yes, baked in

The closest analog is in-toto attestations in software supply-chain land — except in-toto is per-artifact (each release gets one attestation), while ABNG is per-event (every observation, every weight update).

What this enables

  • Forensic post-incident analysis — “Was this model trained on the bad data?” Replay the chain, check.
  • Regulatory replay — the model’s full training history is one cryptographically-linked blob.
  • Reproducibility certification — papers ship (model.snap, dataset) pairs; reviewers reproduce + verify chain in one byte comparison.
  • ML supply chain attestation — fine-tuning extends the chain; provenance of every weight is traceable.
  • Insider-threat detection — anyone modifying historical events breaks every subsequent hash, visible to anyone running verify_chain.

This is closer to Git’s content-addressed model than to traditional ML metadata stores. MLflow / Weights & Biases give you external logs you have to trust; ABNG gives you a model artifact whose history is cryptographically self-attesting.

Capability 4 — Cryptographic dataset attestation

Demo: Lineage attestation with synthetic clinical-trial data (tests/test_abng_lineage_attestation_cjcl.rs + lineage_source.rs)

What this demo proves

The headline test (lineage_attestation_three_signals_independent) proves that tampering one row of a 64-row dataset (changing patient #17’s response from 0.525 to 0.999) produces three independent SHA-256 divergences:

  1. chain_head divergence — every audit event’s hash cascades from row 17 onward.
  2. BLR state_hash divergence — the leaf where the tampered row routes has a different posterior.
  3. provenance_stamp divergence — the dataset fingerprint differs by construction.

The “independent” qualifier is doing real work. Even if an attacker forged one signal (e.g., recomputed the chain to hide the change), the other two would still surface the tamper.

The threat model and why three signals matter

The classic attack against ML provenance is collusion: the attacker controls the training process, so they can produce a consistent-but-fake audit trail. Three independent signals raise the cost:

Attack Defeats which signal? Defeats all three?
Modify training data, recompute chain chain_head ✓ No — BLR state diverges
Modify training data, retrain to match BLR state chain_head ✓, BLR state ✓ No — provenance stamp diverges
Modify training data, retrain, restamp with new fingerprint All three forgeable internally But: fingerprint is publicly verifiable against the dataset bytes

The provenance stamp is sha256(canonical_dataset_bytes). An auditor with the original dataset can recompute this themselves — the attacker can’t forge it without also forging the dataset (which is what we’re trying to detect in the first place). The chain and BLR state are internal consistency checks; the provenance stamp is the external binding to the dataset.

Why a synthetic clinical-trial workload

  • Maps directly to a real industry threat model. “Lab A trains a model claiming approved dataset; attacker substitutes contaminated dataset” is the FDA model-attestation problem in one sentence.
  • 64-row size is realistic for Phase II clinical-trial cohorts (often <200 patients per arm).
  • Cubic dose-response curve y = 0.2 + 0.6·dose + 0.1·dose² is biologically plausible AND exactly fittable in the BLR basis [1, dose, dose², dose³], giving us ground-truth assertions in addition to the security claims.
  • Tamper at row 17 specifically because dose=0.5 falls right at a codebook bin boundary — routing the tampered observation to a specific leaf, making the BLR state_hash divergence clearly attributable.

Data scale: Two graphs trained on 16-row datasets each (the CJC-Lang version of the demo uses 16 rows for interpreter speed; the Rust-side version uses 64). Tampering one row out of 16 — or one out of 64 — is the minimum-effort attack. A 1.5%-of-dataset perturbation is the strongest test possible: if even this is detectable, larger tampers are detected a fortiori. Scale-up to 10⁴ - 10⁶ row clinical-trial datasets is straightforward (the cryptographic argument is dataset-size-invariant) but not yet validated end-to-end with a real anonymized clinical-trial corpus.

Code sketch

fn train_and_stamp(seed: i64, rows: Any, fingerprint: String) -> i64 {
    let g = build_lineage_graph(seed);
    abng_stamp_provenance(g, 0, fingerprint);                // sha256(dataset_bytes)
    let n = len(rows);
    let i = 0;
    while i < n {
        let row = rows[i];
        let dose = row[0];
        let resp = row[1];
        let leaf = route_leaf(g, dose);
        let phi = Tensor.from_vec(
            [1.0, dose, dose * dose, dose * dose * dose], [1, 4]);
        abng_blr_update(g, leaf, phi, Tensor.from_vec([resp], [1]));
        abng_observe(g, leaf, resp);
        i = i + 1;
    }
    g
}

The two graphs g_a and g_b are built identically — only the row data and fingerprint differ. Comparing (chain_head, blr_state_hash[node 3], provenance_stamp) between them yields three different hashes.

Connection to existing techniques

Technique Cryptographic ML-integrated Per-prediction
MLflow / W&B logs No Yes No
Sigstore / in-toto Yes No (artifact-only) No
Datasheet for Datasets (Gebru et al. 2018) No (process-only) Yes No
Model Cards (Mitchell et al. 2019) No Yes No
MLOps governance frameworks Variable Yes Manual
ABNG provenance chain Yes Yes Yes (baked into predict_snap)

ABNG’s contribution is the combination: cryptographic, ML-integrated, AND per-prediction. None of the existing tools do all three in one artifact.

What this enables

Use case Concrete flow
FDA model attestation (pharma) Lab runs trial → trains ABNG → stamps with sha256(approved_dataset). Regulator: cjcl abng explain --model X.snap pred.snap checks all three signals in one CLI invocation
Financial model audit (SEC, Basel III) “Prove this risk model used only data through model-validation cutoff” — chain head + dataset fingerprint answer in one byte comparison
HIPAA-compliant healthcare ML Patient-consent boundaries enforced cryptographically: stamp = sha256(consent_set_v2_canonicalized)
GDPR Article 22 Right to explanation = “show me the audit trail of training data that produced this decision about me”
Academic reproducibility paper.pdf + model.snap + dataset.csv — anyone with all three can verify byte-for-byte
Insider-threat in ML supply chain Attacker who fine-tunes a base model on contaminated data CAN’T hide the audit-chain extension

The closest non-ABNG analog is in-toto-attested ML pipelines (an active research area, see Torres-Arias et al. 2019). The difference: ABNG bakes attestation inside the model artifact, not as a sidecar.

Capability 5 — OOD detection composite

Demo: Asymmetric codebook with deliberately-missing children (tests/test_abng_ood_detection_cjcl.rs + ood_source.rs)

What this demo proves

The headline assertion is three-tier separation:

max(ood_score in dense bins)  <  ood_score in sparse bin  <  ood_score in routing-fall-off bin

Three evidence regimes are cleanly partitioned. The composite is a function of three independent inputs.

The math

ABNG’s ood_score composites three signals:

where:

Signal Formula What it detects
density(x) where Distance from training cluster mean (per-leaf)
epistemic_z(x) Calibrated BLR uncertainty (per-leaf)
prefix_distance(x) Routing-failure rate (per-graph)

The composite combines these into a single bounded [0, 1] score. Each signal catches a different OOD mode:

  • Density catches inputs that fall in a known leaf but far from its training cluster.
  • Epistemic catches inputs where the BLR posterior says “I’m uncertain here.”
  • Prefix distance catches inputs that don’t fit the codebook structure at all — i.e. the descent fell off the tree because no child exists at that prefix byte.

Why deliberately omit one child

A symmetric setup (children in all 4 bins, varying training density) would only exercise the density and epistemic signals — not prefix_distance. By deliberately not adding a child for bin 3, queries in bin 3 descend with matched_prefix=0, and the prefix-distance contribution to the composite fires.

The demo asserts the composite works, not just individual signals. If it only varied density, you’d be left wondering whether the routing-distance contribution is real or just a docstring claim. The asymmetric child set proves it’s real.

Data scale: 32 dense observations per bin on bins 0 and 1, 4 sparse on bin 2, 0 on bin 3 (no leaf) — 68 total density observations. 4 probe queries (one per bin). Sufficient to demonstrate the three-tier separation property; insufficient to characterize behavior under realistic ML inputs (e.g., 1024-D image embeddings, NLP token vectors). OOD detection on high-D unstructured inputs is fundamentally outside the codebook-routing paradigm — ABNG’s OOD score requires structured input where the codebook can produce a meaningful prefix encoding.

Code sketch

fn probe_ood(g: i64, x: f64) -> f64 {
    let x_t = Tensor.from_vec([x], [1]);
    let prefix = abng_encode_prefix(g, x_t);
    let evidence = abng_descend(g, prefix);
    let matched = int(evidence.get([0]));      // matched_prefix
    let leaf = int(evidence.get([1]));         // resolved leaf id
    let phi = features_1d(x);
    abng_ood_score(g, leaf, phi, matched, 1)   // composite [0, 1]
}

// Build phase: add children for bins 0, 1, 2 — NOT bin 3.
abng_add_node(g, 0, 0);   // bin 0 child
abng_add_node(g, 0, 1);   // bin 1 child
abng_add_node(g, 0, 2);   // bin 2 child
// (no add_node for byte 3 — bin 3 queries descend with matched_prefix = 0)

// Train density on bins 0 and 1 heavily, bin 2 lightly:
train_leaf_density(g, 1, 0.125, 32);   // bin 0: dense
train_leaf_density(g, 2, 0.375, 32);   // bin 1: dense
train_leaf_density(g, 3, 0.625, 4);    // bin 2: sparse
// bin 3: no training (no leaf)

After training, probing at x = 0.10 (bin 0, dense) gives a low ood; x = 0.40 (bin 1, dense) low; x = 0.62 (bin 2, sparse) medium; x = 0.90 (bin 3, no leaf) highest.

Connection to existing techniques

Technique OOD signal Bayesian-aware?
Mahalanobis distance OOD (Lee et al. 2018) Distance from class means No
Energy-based OOD (Liu et al. 2020) -logsumexp(logits) No
Deep ensemble disagreement Variance across M models Approximately
ODIN (Liang et al. 2018) Temperature + input perturbation No
Conformal prediction p-values Distribution-free coverage No
ABNG composite density + epistemic + routing Yes (BLR posterior is principled)

The closest is Mahalanobis OOD — both compute a distance-from-training-cluster score. ABNG adds the routing-failure signal on top, which catches a class of inputs Mahalanobis can’t distinguish (in-cluster but topologically out-of-routing).

What this enables

  • Self-driving / robotics — abstain on inputs unfamiliar to the training distribution; hand off to safe-mode.
  • Medical diagnosis — don’t predict on patient profiles outside the training population.
  • Fraud detection — novel attack patterns trigger high OOD scores.
  • Active learning — high-OOD inputs are highest-value labeling candidates.
  • Safety-critical ML — defense-in-depth against distribution shift at inference time, not just monitoring.

A query can be OOD because (a) it’s far from training points (density), (b) the model is uncertain there (epistemic), or (c) it doesn’t fit the codebook structure at all (prefix). Each tells a different operational story — and the composite collapses them into one actionable score.

Capability 6 — Adaptive structural triggers

Demo: Two-sibling shared-signature topology (tests/test_abng_adaptive_triggers_cjcl.rs + adaptive_source.rs)

What this demo proves

After running abng_decide_step three times on a graph with two sibling children:

  1. merge_count == 1 — exactly one Merge action fires (deterministic given the canary thresholds).
  2. audit_log_grew — the structural mutation is recorded as an event in the chain.
  3. verify_chain == Ok — the chain integrity holds after structural mutation.
  4. chain_head matches the Rust-side decide_step_canary BYTE-FOR-BYTE — this is the most striking property in the entire demo set.

That last point deserves emphasis. The Rust-side decide_step_canary_tests.rs locks chain_head = d064fb08…7807. The CJC-Lang demo, running through both AST eval AND MIR exec, produces the same hex. The same workload, expressed three different ways (direct Rust API, CJC-Lang AST, CJC-Lang MIR), produces bit-identical SHA-256 output through 23+ events.

The math behind the Merge trigger

decide_step evaluates each node against six trigger conditions in order: Compress → Merge → Split → Prune → Grow → Freeze. For the Merge trigger specifically, ABNG checks two conditions on a pair of siblings (i, j):

  1. Signature similarity: Hamming distance of the 8-byte truncated SHA-256 signatures < tau_merge. The signature is computed from a 4-channel Welford profile (prediction, uncertainty, calibration, routing). Similar profiles → similar signatures → low Hamming distance.

  2. Posterior similarity (KL divergence): where (μᵢ, Σᵢ) are the BLR posterior parameters at node i. This is the standard multivariate Gaussian KL.

Both conditions must hold for Merge to fire. The Merge action then folds j’s observations into i via Chan/Golub/LeVeque parallel Welford merge — preserving the post-merge stats as if they’d been observed in series.

Why the two-sibling shared-signature topology

  • Mirrors the Rust-side decide_step_canary exactly — root + 2 children at keys 1 and 2, three observations at root (0.10, 0.25, 0.40), three decide_step calls. This lets the CJC-Lang demo serve as a direct cross-pipeline determinism gate.
  • Merge is the most reliably-triggered action. With identical training distributions on the two siblings, their signatures align trivially (Hamming distance = 0). Decision-policy thresholds tau_merge=0 and kl_merge=0.1 are satisfied immediately — KL between fresh-prior posteriors is exactly zero.
  • Small graph keeps the test fast — 4 nodes, 3 observations, 3 decide_step calls. Runs in milliseconds even through the AST interpreter.

Data scale: 3 observations + 3 decide_step calls, on a 4-node graph. This is deliberately the smallest demo in the entire suite — its job is to prove the trigger semantics fire deterministically and that the chain_head matches the Rust-side canary byte-for-byte. It’s not a demonstration that ABNG-driven structural adaptation is competitive against e.g. Mondrian Forests on 10⁵-row tabular benchmarks. That comparison is genuine future work — engineering a Grow-firing or Split-firing workload at scale, then measuring vs. baseline tree learners.

Code sketch

fn main() {
    let g = build_adaptive_graph(42);

    abng_observe(g, 0, 0.10);
    abng_observe(g, 0, 0.25);
    abng_observe(g, 0, 0.40);

    let i = 0;
    while i < 3 {
        let counts = abng_decide_step(g);    // [grow, split, merge, prune, compress, freeze]
        i = i + 1;
    }

    let merge_count = abng_action_count(g, 2);  // → 1
    print("merge_count: " + to_string(merge_count));
    print("chain_head: " + abng_chain_head(g));  // → d064fb08…7807
}

Connection to existing techniques

Technique Adaptive structure? Triggers Domain
Mondrian Forests (Lakshminarayanan 2014) Yes (stochastic splits) Online Tabular
Bayesian Additive Regression Trees (Chipman 2010) Yes (stochastic) Batch Tabular
Hoeffding Trees / VFDT (Domingos 2000) Yes (statistical) Streaming Tabular
Concept-drift adaptive learners (ADWIN, Page-Hinkley) Yes (drift-triggered) Streaming Various
Neural Architecture Search (Zoph 2017) Yes (optimization-based) Offline Deep learning
ABNG decide_step Yes (deterministic, audit-tracked) Six action types Bayesian belief graphs

The closest analog is Hoeffding Trees — both fire structural changes based on running statistics. The differences: Hoeffding Trees use Hoeffding-bound-based split tests; ABNG uses fixed thresholds on a 14-element decision-policy tensor. ABNG also adds the audit-chain integration that lets you prove what structural changes happened and when.

What this enables

  • Self-organizing ML models — graph capacity scales with workload complexity; no manual architecture design.
  • Streaming / online learning — non-stationary distributions cause Grow on new modes, Prune on dead branches.
  • Memory-efficient ML — Compress unused subtrees; Merge redundant leaves; Prune inactive nodes.
  • Hierarchical model distillation — stable subtrees can be Frozen and exported as compiled MLPs.
  • Continual learning without catastrophic forgetting — Freeze stable-signature subtrees while training others.
  • AutoML topology search — decision-policy thresholds are hyperparameters; the trigger ladder is the search algorithm.
Warning

Honest caveat: the demo proves Merge fires deterministically. The other five trigger types (Grow, Split, Prune, Compress, Freeze) are tested at the primitive level but not demonstrated firing in a realistic workload. The “graph adapts to workload complexity” claim is half-substantiated by current demos. Closing this gap is straightforward (more demos with engineered workloads that fire each trigger type) but not yet shipped.

Capability 7 — Calibration / ECE

Demo: Hand-constructed well-calibrated vs miscalibrated outcomes (tests/test_abng_calibration_cjcl.rs + calibration_source.rs)

What this demo proves

Two graphs receive 30 calibration observations each. Same predicted probabilities (p ∈ {0.1, 0.5, 0.9}, 10 each), different outcome distributions:

Graph Outcome convention Expected ECE
A: well-calibrated predicted=0.1 → 1 of 10 positive; predicted=0.9 → 9 of 10 positive < 0.05
B: miscalibrated predicted=0.1 → 9 of 10 positive (FLIPPED); predicted=0.9 → 1 of 10 positive > 0.5

The headline assertion ece_a < ece_b separates the two cleanly. Plus the specific bounds: ece_a < 0.05 (well-calibrated achieves near-zero ECE) and ece_b > 0.5 (flipped outcomes produce huge ECE).

The math

ABNG uses standard 15-bin reliability-binning ECE (Naeini, Cooper, Hauskrecht 2015):

where:

  • B_b is the set of predictions falling in bin b (15 equal-width bins on [0, 1])
  • accuracy(B_b) = fraction of correct predictions in bin b
  • confidence(B_b) = mean predicted probability in bin b
  • |B_b| / N = bin’s empirical weight

A perfectly calibrated model has accuracy(B_b) = confidence(B_b) for every populated bin → ECE = 0. The miscalibrated graph with flipped outcomes pushes accuracy ≈ 1 - confidence in each bin → ECE saturates near 0.8.

Why “flip outcomes” as the miscalibration model

  • Maximum-ECE case. Predicting 90% confidence and being right 10% of the time is the worst possible outcome — confidence is anti-correlated with truth. This makes the test gate ece_b > 0.5 reliable; small numerical jitter doesn’t push the result close to the threshold.
  • Same prediction distribution → isolates the calibration signal. Both graphs see the same (p, y) pairs modulo y. Any difference in ECE is attributable to outcomes, not to predictions or to bin populations.
  • Three reliability bins (0.1, 0.5, 0.9) span low/mid/high probability range without requiring 100+ observations to populate every bin.

Data scale: 30 calibration observations per graph (2 graphs total = 60 observations). This is the minimum sufficient sample size to make the three-bin reliability statistics non-trivially populated and the ECE separation ece_a < 0.05 < 0.5 < ece_b reliable. It does not validate calibration on actual classification tasks (image classifiers, transformer logits, etc.) — those workloads have thousands of (p, y) pairs and 10-100 calibration buckets. Per-leaf ECE on a real classifier is straightforward future work.

Code sketch

fn well_calibrated_run(g: i64, node: i64) {
    // For predicted_prob = 0.1: 1 positive, 9 negative (10% base rate).
    let i = 0;
    while i < 10 {
        if i < 1 { abng_calibration_observe(g, node, 0.1, true); }
        if i >= 1 { abng_calibration_observe(g, node, 0.1, false); }
        i = i + 1;
    }
    // ... same pattern for p=0.5 (5/10) and p=0.9 (9/10) ...
}

fn miscalibrated_run(g: i64, node: i64) {
    // For predicted_prob = 0.1: 9 positive, 1 negative (FLIPPED).
    // For predicted_prob = 0.9: 1 positive, 9 negative (FLIPPED).
    // ... see source for full pattern ...
}

let ece_a = abng_calibration_ece(g_a, 0);     // < 0.05
let ece_b = abng_calibration_ece(g_b, 0);     // > 0.5

Connection to existing techniques

Technique Calibration Per-leaf?
Platt scaling (Platt 1999) Post-hoc logistic regression on logits No (global)
Isotonic regression (Zadrozny 2002) Monotonic piecewise-constant fit No (global)
Temperature scaling (Guo et al. 2017) Single-parameter softmax rescale No (global)
Beta calibration (Kull et al. 2017) Beta distribution fit No (global)
Histogram binning (Zadrozny 2001) Per-bin frequency estimate Possible per-leaf
ABNG calibration 15-bin reliability + ECE Yes (per-node)

ABNG’s calibration is per-node — different leaves can be independently calibrated. This matters in practice: a model can be well-calibrated for European customers but miscalibrated for Asian customers (different leaves of the codebook). Per-leaf ECE makes this visible; global ECE would average it out.

What this enables

  • Risk-sensitive deployment — a poorly-calibrated 95%-confident prediction is operationally indistinguishable from an honest 60%-confident one — but you’ll act on it differently.
  • Threshold tuning — “should I act on predictions above 0.7?” depends on whether 0.7-bin predictions are actually correct 70% of the time.
  • Model selection in production — beyond accuracy/F1: ECE is the second-order quality metric.
  • Active learning targeting — high-ECE bins are evidence the model needs more training data in that probability range.
  • Per-segment calibration analysis — “is my model better calibrated for some user segments than others?” is answerable in one query.

Capability 8 — Distribution-drift detection

Demo: Mean shift from 0 to 0.8 on a 4-D density tracker (tests/test_abng_drift_detection_cjcl.rs + drift_source.rs)

What this demo proves

A three-phase workload: train baseline, freeze, observe under same distribution, observe under shifted distribution. Three assertions:

  1. drift_grew — drift_score(post-shift) > drift_score(same-dist).
  2. meaningful_shift — drift_score(post-shift) > 2 × drift_score(same-dist). Catches a regression where drift detection becomes noisy.
  3. drift_nonneg — both scores are non-negative (no NaN, no negative noise).

The math

ABNG’s drift detection is built on a streaming diagonal-Gaussian density tracker. For features φ ∈ ℝᵈ, the tracker maintains:

  • Running mean: μ̂ updated via Welford
  • Running diagonal covariance: Σ̂_diag updated via Welford on (φᵢ - μ̂)²

freeze_drift_baseline(node) snapshots (μ̂_baseline, Σ̂_diag_baseline) at a reference point (typically end of pre-deployment training). After freezing, drift_score measures the divergence between the current tracker and the frozen baseline:

— a weighted combination of mean shift (under baseline covariance) and covariance ratio deviation. Pure mean shift (this demo’s case) gives a score dominated by the first term.

Why a pure mean shift

  • Translation, not rescaling isolates the mean-shift contribution to drift_score. A combined “scale and rotate” shift would test more, but the basic mean-shift case is the simplest sufficient demonstration.
  • 0 to 0.8 is large enough to be unambiguous. Both distributions have ~0.01 jitter; shifting the center by 0.8 is 80x the jitter — the drift signal saturates well above the floor.
  • Same density-tracker geometry — same dimension count (4-D features), same covariance scale, same batch sizes (16 each post-baseline). Only the location changes.

Data scale: 32 baseline samples + 16 same-distribution + 16 shifted = 64 density observations total, on a single node. This proves the drift_score machinery responds correctly to a controlled mean shift; it does not characterize behavior under realistic concept drift (gradual drift, abrupt drift, recurring drift, periodic shift). Production ML drift detection is typically validated on streaming corpora with millions of points across months of real customer behavior — that’s a workload-grade benchmark, not a research demo, and it’s explicitly future work.

Code sketch

let g = build_drift_graph(7);

// Phase 0: train baseline density on N(0, 0.01).
let baseline_batch = density_batch(0.0, 32);
abng_density_observe(g, 0, baseline_batch);

abng_freeze_drift_baseline(g, 0);     // snapshot reference state

// Phase A: same distribution.
let same_batch = density_batch(0.0, 16);
abng_density_observe(g, 0, same_batch);
let drift_low = abng_drift_score(g, 0);

// Phase B: shifted distribution N(0.8, 0.01).
let shifted_batch = density_batch(0.8, 16);
abng_density_observe(g, 0, shifted_batch);
let drift_high = abng_drift_score(g, 0);

// drift_high > 2 * drift_low (the demo's headline assertion)

Connection to existing techniques

Technique Statistic Streaming? Reference required
Kolmogorov-Smirnov test Max CDF difference No Yes
Population Stability Index (PSI) Discrete distribution distance No (batch) Yes
Maximum Mean Discrepancy (Gretton 2012) Kernel-based difference Possible Yes
Page-Hinkley test Change-point detection Yes Implicit
ADWIN (Bifet 2007) Adaptive windowing Yes None (auto-detect)
DDM (Gama 2004) Drift Detection Method (error rate) Yes None
ABNG drift_score Mahalanobis-style on density tracker Streaming Frozen baseline

The closest analog is PSI (widely used in finance) — both compute a deviation-from-baseline distribution score. The differences: PSI is for discrete histograms; ABNG operates on continuous diagonal-Gaussian density. PSI is computed batch; ABNG accumulates streamingly via Welford.

What this enables

  • Production ML monitoring — alert ops when input distribution drifts from training; trigger retraining.
  • Continual learning auto-trigger — drift signal is the cue to incorporate new data into the model.
  • A/B test data validation — confirm the new dataset’s distribution matches the original before deploying a new model.
  • Sensor / pipeline integrity — detect upstream data-source switches, sensor drift, schema changes.
  • Customer-behavior modeling — recommender systems trained on Q1 data drifting against Q3 user behavior.

Capability 9 — Log compaction

Demo: Multi-node observation stream + compact_log (tests/test_abng_compact_log_cjcl.rs + compact_source.rs)

What this demo proves

Five training observations across three nodes, then compact_log fires:

  1. emitted == 3 — exactly one StatsSnapshot per touched node.
  2. audit_grew_by_emitted — audit_post == audit_pre + emitted.
  3. chain_advanced — chain_head changed (events were properly chain-linked).
  4. verify_chain == Ok post-compact — the new events don’t break integrity.

The math (and what’s deferred)

compact_log(g, until_seq) walks events in [0, until_seq), tracks the set of distinct node IDs touched, and for each emits one StatsSnapshot event whose payload is (node_id, sha256(canonical_bytes(node.stats))).

This sets up the read-side optimization that ships in a future phase: when smart_replay encounters a StatsSnapshot, it can:

  1. Trust the snapshot’s stats_hash matches the node’s canonical_bytes (consistency check, shipped today).
  2. Skip the node.observe(value) mutation for subsequent BeliefUpdate events in the same node’s pre-snapshot range, since the snapshot subsumes their cumulative effect.
  3. Install the stats directly from the per-node section’s canonical_bytes instead of replaying from observations.

The shipped layer is the marker emission + chain-integrity preservation + smart_replay consistency check. The cycle-saving fast-forward is Phase 0.6 work.

Code sketch

abng_observe(g, 0, 0.1);
abng_observe(g, 0, 0.2);
abng_observe(g, 1, 0.5);
abng_observe(g, 1, 0.6);
abng_observe(g, 2, 0.9);

let audit_pre = abng_audit_len(g);             // e.g. 8
let emitted = abng_compact_log(g, audit_pre);  // → 3 (3 touched nodes)
let audit_post = abng_audit_len(g);            // 8 + 3 = 11
print("verify_chain: " + to_string(abng_verify_chain(g)));  // → true

Data scale: 5 observations across 3 nodes (smallest in the suite). This proves the marker emission is deterministic and chain-integrity-preserving. The interesting scale claim — “compact_log + smart-replay can fast-forward replay of a 10⁶-event audit log” — is deferred to Phase 0.6 along with the actual cycle-saving optimization.

Connection to existing techniques

Technique Mechanism Domain
Database WAL compaction Replace old WAL records with snapshot RDBMS
LSM tree compaction Merge sorted runs KV stores (RocksDB, Cassandra)
Chandy-Lamport snapshotting Distributed-system state capture Distributed systems
Checkpoint-restart Periodic full-state save HPC, ML training
ABNG compact_log + StatsSnapshot Event-log markers, hash-chain-integrated ML training audit logs

The closest analog is WAL log compaction in databases — periodic snapshot points that let you truncate older log entries without losing recoverability. ABNG adds the chain-integrity property: the snapshot itself is a hash-linked event, so post-compaction you can still cryptographically verify nothing was tampered with.

What this enables

  • Long-running training without log explosion — periodic compaction preserves checkpoints while bounding audit-log growth.
  • Snapshot-based debugging — each StatsSnapshot is a “save point” for forensic replay.
  • Foundation for the Phase 0.6 fast-forward optimization — smart-replay can fast-forward past observation runs by trusting the StatsSnapshot.
  • Distributed training checkpointing — workers compact-log periodically; central server replays the compact form.
Warning

The cycle-saving optimization is deferred to Phase 0.6. Today the marker layer ships with chain-integrity preservation; the actual fast-forward that would make this user-visibly faster lands later.

Capability 10 — Maturity observability

Demo: Stable observation stream + decide_step (tests/test_abng_maturity_inspection_cjcl.rs + maturity_source.rs)

What this demo proves

Read abng_node_maturity at two timepoints: fresh graph (T=0) and after 10 stable observations + 5 decide_step calls (T=1). Five assertions:

  1. sum_at_t0 == 0.0 — fresh graph, all maturity flags are zero.
  2. maturity_evolved — sum at T=1 strictly exceeds sum at T=0.
  3. calib_binary AND unc_binary — positions [1] (calibration_stable) and [2] (uncertainty_stable) are strict 0/1 flags.
  4. samples_seen_nonneg — position [0] (samples_seen) is non-negative.
  5. trust_in_range — position [3] (trust_level) is in [0, 1].

The semantics

abng_node_maturity(g, node) returns a 4-element f64 tensor:

Index Field Type Definition
0 samples_seen non-negative count Total observations applied to this node
1 calibration_stable binary 0/1 True if 3-window ECE history’s \|Δ\| < ece_stability_max_delta
2 uncertainty_stable binary 0/1 True if 3-window σ history shows ratio < sigma_stability_ratio
3 trust_level continuous [0, 1] Composite trust score from signature stability + maturity flags

The flags advance through decide_step calls plus the underlying signature_stable_calls counter that increments on consecutive matching signatures.

Why a stable observation stream

The maturity flags advance under two conditions:

  • signature_stable_calls increments when consecutive decide_step calls observe an unchanged signature
  • samples_seen simply counts observations

By choosing identical observations (abng_observe(g, n, 0.1) repeatedly), the signature stays exactly identical across decide_step calls — the stability counter climbs deterministically. With 10 observations + 5 decide_step calls, at least the stability flags should flip.

Realistic data (varying observations) would also work but with lower predictability — the signature might wobble enough that signature_stable_calls resets occasionally. The stable-stream choice makes the demo deterministic.

Data scale: 10 observations + 5 decide_step calls. Deliberately tiny — the workload exists to prove “maturity flags evolve from 0 → 1 under stable training,” not to characterize maturity dynamics under realistic noisy ML training. A real production-monitoring deployment would observe maturity over thousands of decide_step calls across hours/days of training.

Code sketch

let g = build_maturity_graph(42);

let mat0 = abng_node_maturity(g, 0);  // [0, 0, 0, 0] — fresh

let i = 0;
while i < 10 {
    abng_observe(g, 0, 0.1);
    abng_observe(g, 1, 0.1);
    i = i + 1;
}
let j = 0;
while j < 5 {
    abng_decide_step(g);
    j = j + 1;
}

let mat1 = abng_node_maturity(g, 0);
let r0 = mat1.get([0]);  // samples_seen (count > 0)
let r1 = mat1.get([1]);  // calibration_stable (0 or 1)
let r2 = mat1.get([2]);  // uncertainty_stable (0 or 1)
let r3 = mat1.get([3]);  // trust_level (continuous [0, 1])

Connection to existing techniques

Technique Convergence diagnostic Domain
Gelman-Rubin R̂ Cross-chain variance ratio MCMC convergence
Effective Sample Size (ESS) Autocorrelation-based MCMC convergence
Validation-loss plateau detection Loss derivative threshold Deep learning training
EarlyStopping callback Patience + monitor metric sklearn/keras training
ML monitoring stacks (Arize, WhyLabs) External time-series dashboards Production ML
ABNG node_maturity Per-node 4-tuple Bayesian belief graphs

The closest analog in ML practice is EarlyStopping + production monitoring — both ask “has my model converged enough to deploy?” ABNG’s contribution: per-node granularity (you can see which leaves have stabilized) and cryptographic integrity (the maturity values are derived from the same audit-tracked state, not an external log).

What this enables

  • Production ML monitoring dashboard — “has my model converged?” → check maturity per-node.
  • Auto-stop training — stop when maturity flags have all stabilized (avoid wasted compute).
  • Hyperparameter tuning — which learning rate makes flags settle fastest?
  • Convergence-failure debugging — which flag never reaches 1? — points at the failing component.
  • Production rollout gating — deploy-time check: only ship models where calibration_stable == 1.
  • Federated learning aggregation — aggregate worker updates only from nodes where maturity has stabilized.

Capability 11 — End-to-end determinism

Demo: Universal property — every CJC-Lang demo asserts it.

What every demo proves

Every CJC-Lang demo runs through both cjc-eval (AST tree-walk) AND cjc-mir-exec (MIR register-machine), then asserts byte-equal printed output. Across the 13 ABNG demo files, this constitutes ~95 byte-equal parity assertions over a vocabulary of ~30 abng_* builtins.

The strongest single instance: the adaptive triggers demo’s chain_head matches the Rust-side decide_step_canary byte-for-byte (d064fb08…7807). Same workload, three pipelines (direct Rust API + AST eval + MIR exec), bit-identical SHA-256 over 23+ audit events.

The architectural choices that make this work

Three load-bearing decisions enforced across the workspace:

  1. Kahan compensated summation for all FP reductions (KahanAccumulatorF64). Naive Σxᵢ accumulates rounding error in O(n·ε); Kahan reduces this to O(ε). More importantly, Kahan is order-deterministic for fixed input order — no SIMD-introduced reordering, no FMA fusion. This is the single biggest source of “approximately deterministic” failure in standard ML stacks.

  2. BTreeMap/BTreeSet only, never HashMap. Hash maps with random hashing have iteration order that depends on the per-process seed — different runs can iterate in different orders, and any code that depends on iteration order (e.g., reduction loops) becomes non-deterministic. BTreeMap iterates in sorted key order, every time.

  3. SplitMix64 with explicit seed threading. No thread-local randomness, no time-based seeds, no platform-specific entropy. Every random draw is reproducible from (initial_seed, operation_history).

These three choices aren’t aspirational — they’re enforced by the parity gates. If anyone introduces FMA in cjc-runtime/linalg.rs tomorrow, every parity test fails simultaneously with byte-divergent output.

What this means for ML practitioners

ML reproducibility is famously hard. A typical “deterministic” PyTorch model can produce different bytes across runs due to:

  • CUDA atomic ops (non-deterministic reduction order on GPU)
  • cuDNN algorithm selection (can pick different kernels per run)
  • Multi-threaded CPU reductions (thread-scheduling non-determinism)
  • FMA fusion (compiler-dependent instruction selection)
  • Hash-randomization in Python’s dict

Each of these has been a real production-incident root cause. ABNG’s parity gates prove the absence of these bugs every test run.

Connection to the reproducibility crisis literature

Source Claim
Pineau et al. 2021, “Improving Reproducibility in ML Research” Most ML papers can’t be reproduced even with the same code
Hutson 2018, “Artificial intelligence faces reproducibility crisis” Structural problem in the field
TensorFlow Determinism Project (tf.config.experimental.enable_op_determinism) Best-effort, with known gaps
PyTorch determinism guide Documents environmental flags; doesn’t guarantee bit-equality
ML reproducibility checklists (NeurIPS 2019+) Process-level requirements
ABNG parity gates Cryptographic byte-equality across pipelines

ABNG’s contribution to this conversation: byte-equality is achievable if you commit to determinism-by-construction at every layer (no FMA, no random hash, no thread-local RNG). The parity gates prove it run by run.

What this enables

  • Reproducibility — same seed → same model bytes; papers ship verifiable results.
  • Cross-platform deployment — train on Linux, deploy on Windows → byte-identical model (modulo cross-platform CI verification, which is the next piece of work).
  • Distributed training — deterministic operations enable provable consistency without per-step coordination.
  • Regulatory replay — regulator can re-run training and verify every byte.
  • CI / regression detection — byte-equal output makes test-failure debugging trivial — diff lines, not “it’s flaky.”
  • Differential testing across executor versions — new optimizer? Old vs new must produce same bytes for all valid programs.
Warning

Cross-platform determinism is not yet verified. All canary hashes were computed on Windows. They should hold on Linux/macOS (Kahan, no FMA, BTreeMap), but this isn’t yet tested in CI. Adding GitHub Actions CI that runs the suite on three platforms and compares the canaries is a one-day project — and one of the first items on the Phase 0.6 list.

Phase 0.5 in numbers

The current ABNG state is the result of Phase 0.5, which shipped:

Item What it added
v12 wire-format bump Per-node provenance_stamp_hash field + new 0x1C ProvenanceStamped audit kind; NodeStats::canonical_bytes extended 24B → 32B to capture the Kahan compensation register
smart_replay API Opt-in tamper detection via StatsSnapshot consistency check (cycle-saving fast-forward layer deferred to Phase 0.6)
TOML --config files for cjcl abng train Hand-rolled minimal TOML parser (zero external deps), full schema for graph / codebook / leaf_head / blr_prior / density / calibration / decision_policy / training / output sections
Chess RL v2.6 retrofit scaffold Sibling test file demonstrating the API pattern; locked v2.6 weight-hash canary
6 capability demos OOD detection, adaptive triggers, calibration, drift, compact_log, maturity — all in CJC-Lang source with AST↔︎MIR parity gates
3 application demos PINN, tabular GP-like, lineage attestation — both Rust-direct AND CJC-Lang versions

Net impact: 906 → 960 tests, +54 in this final round, 0 failures across the whole suite.

What’s next — and why interpreter / compiler work matters

ABNG is genuinely useful for a specific niche: regulated ML training where you need Bayesian uncertainty + cryptographically-verifiable lineage on tabular data. The demos prove the niche capabilities; they don’t prove ABNG should replace PyTorch.

Three things stand between current ABNG and production readiness:

Cross-platform determinism CI

The locked canary hashes are Windows-only today. They should hold across platforms (the architecture is designed for it), but this is unverified. Adding GitHub Actions CI that runs the full test suite on Linux + macOS + Windows and compares the locked canaries is a one-day project that pays off forever — every future commit either preserves cross-platform determinism or fails CI loudly.

Performance benchmarks at scale

This is the biggest single gap between the current state and production readiness, and it deserves emphasis: none of the demos in this post test ABNG at production-scale data. Sample sizes range from 3 (adaptive triggers) to 200 (tabular GP-like). Every demo runs in milliseconds. That’s an asset for test-suite reproducibility but a liability for the “ABNG works at scale” story.

What’s specifically not yet validated:

Scale claim Status
“ABNG O(N · d²) beats classical GP O(N³)” Correct by construction, never wall-clock-benchmarked vs SVGP / sklearn / GPy at n ∈ {10³, 10⁴, 10⁵, 10⁶}
“Per-leaf BLR posterior converges to truth on real PDE problems” Demonstrated on 1-D heat eq with 40 samples; never on 2-D Burgers / Navier-Stokes at 10⁴ collocation points
“Lineage attestation works on real anonymized clinical-trial data” Demonstrated on 16-row synthetic; never on a real Phase II/III trial corpus
“Drift detection catches realistic concept drift” Demonstrated on a controlled mean shift; never on production streaming data
“OOD score generalizes to high-D embeddings” Demonstrated on 4-D codebook-routed inputs; fundamentally outside scope for unstructured high-D inputs
“Per-leaf calibration scales to real classifiers” Demonstrated on 30 synthetic (p, y) pairs; never on real classifier outputs at 10³+ predictions

Closing these gaps is the bulk of Phase 0.6 + 0.7 work. Each is a real deliverable: pick a benchmark dataset / workload, write the wall-clock measurement code, run it, ship the numbers. Until that’s done, ABNG is empirically demonstrated at small scale and theoretically expected to hold at larger scale.

Compiler / interpreter performance

This is the bigger long-term constraint. The CJC-Lang interpreter is currently 5–50× slower than direct Rust API access for ABNG demos. For research / experimentation that’s fine. For production ML training at scale, it’s a hard ceiling.

The chess RL benchmark hit this wall directly — the bottleneck for v2.5 was interpreter throughput, not ML algorithm design. Specific levers, in increasing order of effort:

  • MIR optimizer passes — current optimizer has CF + DCE; further passes (loop-invariant code motion, common subexpression elimination, function inlining) are weeks of work each.
  • Hot-path specialization in cjc-runtime — ABNG already shows the pattern (75 native builtins for what users actually do). Has architectural cost: every native kernel is a feature you can’t compose out of simpler primitives.
  • AOT compilation — cjcl compile foo.cjcl → foo.exe. LLVM or Cranelift backend. Months of work but the only path that closes the perf gap meaningfully.

Most likely sequencing: continue ABNG (Phase 0.6 work — smart-replay fast-forward, cross-platform CI, lineage case study) while landing incremental MIR optimizer + native-kernel work. AOT is a multi-month commitment that depends on whether CJC-Lang’s long-term identity is research vehicle or production language.

Note

ABNG lives at crates/cjc-abng/ in the CJC-Lang repository. The 13 demo files are under tests/test_abng_*.rs and tests/abng_demos/. The full test count is 960; the source is open.

item.title: TidyView v3 and the D-HARHT Memory Engine
item.dc:creator: Adam Ezzat
item.description:
🕑 ~25 min read
12phases
495TidyView tests
258cjc-data unit tests
~108ns/op (DHarht Mem)
TL;DR

v0.1.7 ships TidyView v3 — twelve phases of focused optimization to CJC-Lang’s data engine — and a new module cjc_data::detcoll containing five deterministic collection types. The headline win is D-HARHT Memory, a sealed-lookup table that matches std::HashMap speed (~108 ns/op) while preserving byte-equal iteration order across runs. The new SealedU64Map wrapper is the recommended drop-in for any BTreeMap<u64, V> whose access pattern is “build once, look up many” and where reproducibility matters.

The arc

When v0.1.6 shipped TidyView’s adaptive selection engine, I closed the post with a list of “what’s next” items. v0.1.7 is what happened after that list got picked apart, one item at a time, over twelve phases. Each phase fixed a specific bottleneck, shipped a specific bench, and then exposed the next bottleneck.

The phases compound. By Phase 4, joins on categorical keys are 6× faster. By Phase 10, sealed u64 lookup matches HashMap speed. By Phase 12, the determinism contract holds even under adversarial collision-heavy workloads.

This post walks through every phase: what it solved, what it cost, what came next.

Part 1 — TidyView v3

The eight TidyView-specific phases (0 through 6) all share a theme: categorical columns are the right unit of work for analytical queries, but the engine kept paying for String allocations every time a verb touched one. Each phase eliminated one of those allocations on a different verb.

Phase 0 — Tidyverse parity audit

The starting point: verify the dispatch path from .cjcl source → tidy_dispatch → Rust API was 1:1 with no bypass. Six new tests pin filter / select / arrange / distinct / group_by + summarise. No work, just verification — but it set the contract for everything that followed: any optimization had to keep this dispatch layer pure.

Phase 1 — Deterministic Adaptive Dictionary Engine

A new module cjc_data::byte_dict shipped six types: ByteStringPool, ByteStrView, AdaptiveCodes (U8/U16/U32/U64 auto-promoting at 256 / 65 536 / 2³² thresholds), ByteDictionary (BTreeMap<Vec<u8>, u64> lookup, frozen flag, CategoryOrdering::FirstSeen|Lexical|Explicit), CategoricalColumn, UnknownCategoryPolicy.

Six-clause determinism contract: integer-only thresholds, BTreeMap (not HashMap), byte-lex (not Unicode) sort, lazy code-width promotion, strict frozen rejection, byte-equal round-trip.

Standalone — Phase 1 didn’t replace anything. Phase 2 is where it started showing up in production.

Phase 2 — Cat-aware group_by + distinct

When all group_by keys are Column::Categorical, the lookup BTreeMap keys flip from Vec<String> to Vec<u32> codes. Mixed-type keys auto-fall-back to the string path.

The bench that mattered: 1M rows × 100 unique categorical keys, single-key group_by_fast:

Path Time
String-key BTreeMap 859.9 ms
Cat-aware (Vec<u32> keys) 114.8 ms
Speedup 7.49×

The win is purely structural — codes are u32 integers, not heap-allocated String clones per row.

Phase 3 — Hybrid streaming set-op fast paths

AdaptiveSelection::intersect and union got per-chunk dispatch when one or both operands are Hybrid. Pre-Phase-3, those routed through to_verbatim_mask() → AND/OR → re-classify, allocating a nrows/64-word bitmap per Hybrid operand.

The 4×4 chunk-shape table (Empty | All | Sparse(Vec<u16>) | Dense(Box<[u64]>)) collapses to 5 effective shapes after Empty/All folding:

left × right path
Sparse ∩ Sparse merge-walk on two ascending Vec<u16>
Sparse ∩ Dense filter-walk: bit-test each sparse row against partner words
Dense ∩ Dense per-word AND over chunk_len.div_ceil(64) u64s
Empty ∩ * fold to Empty
All ∩ x fold to x unchanged

Headline bench (favorable case, 100k rows, sparse-chunked × dense-chunked Hybrid):

Phase 3 chunked path:           4.69 μs avg (n=100)
Pre-Phase-3 materialize path:  391.41 μs avg (n=100)
Speedup:                        83.46×

Caveat I had to be honest about: that’s the favorable case. Contiguous-Hybrid (Dense × Dense everywhere) shrinks the win to ~5–10× — still net positive but no longer allocation-bound.

Phase 4 — Cat-aware joins

This was the bundled deliverable: cat-aware joins plus Column ↔︎ CategoricalColumn conversions.

The join path: when every join-key column is Column::Categorical on both frames, route through BTreeMap<Vec<u32>, Vec<usize>> with a deterministic per-column right-code → left-code remap. Each frame owns its own dictionary, so left-side code 3 ≠ right-side code 3 in general — the per-column remap is what makes cross-frame comparison safe:

right_to_left[ki][right_code] = Some(left_code) | None

None means the level doesn’t exist on the left dictionary; that right row is skipped before BTreeMap insertion (it can never join). Build order via BTreeMap<&str, u32> over left levels — BTree not Hash, so the remap is itself deterministic.

Headline bench (100k × 100k rows, 100 unique categorical levels, single-key inner join):

Path Time
String-key 16.82 s
Cat-aware 2.77 s
Speedup 6.08×

Phase 5 — Three deferred items in one bundle

Phase 5 was where I closed all three deferrals from prior phases:

(a) Full Column wiring. New Column::CategoricalAdaptive(Box<CategoricalColumn>) variant — a sibling of the legacy Column::Categorical. The 21 column-reader sites all dispatch through a to_legacy_categorical() shim (lossless when levels are UTF-8 + no nulls). Hot paths bypass the shim entirely.

(b) Filter-chain Hybrid intersect path. New predicate_bytecode::evaluate_to_selection. TidyView::filter routes through existing.intersect(fresh) when the existing mask is already Hybrid and not in sparse-path territory — exercising the Phase 3 chunk dispatch in production.

(c) Phase 5 = cat-aware arrange. When Column::Categorical levels are lex-sorted (the Phase 17 forcats invariant), comparing u32 codes is byte-identical to comparing String levels. Pre-resolution per key:

enum ArrangeKeyResolved<'a> {
    CatCodes { codes: &'a [u32], descending: bool },  // pure u32 compare
    Legacy { col: &'a Column, descending: bool },     // string compare
}

100k rows × 100 unique categorical levels, single-key ascending sort:

Path Time
String compare 92.73 ms
u32 code compare 9.58 ms
Speedup 9.68×

Higher than Phase 4’s join win because arrange’s stable sort calls the comparator ~1.6M times for 100k rows — every eliminated String::cmp counts.

Phase 6 — Streaming summarise + cat-aware mutate + lazy optimizer

Streaming summarise: TidyView::summarise_streaming(keys, aggs) walks visible rows ONCE, maintaining a BTreeMap<key, Vec<AccState>> where each accumulator holds running state of constant size:

StreamingAgg AccState Algorithm
Count { n: u64 } counter
Sum(col) { sum, c } Kahan running sum
Mean(col) { sum, c, n } Kahan + count
Min(col) / Max(col) { cur, any } NaN-aware extremum
Var(col) / Sd(col) { n, mean, m2 } Welford online variance

Memory: O(K · acc_size) instead of O(N · usize). For 100M rows / 1000 groups: ~32 KB vs ~800 MB — 25 000× less memory.

CPU savings on 1M × 1000 groups were modest (1.10×, BTreeMap ops dominate at this shape) — but the memory win unlocks workloads that previously OOM’d.

Cat-aware mutate: mutate("k_copy", DExpr::Col("k")) over a categorical column used to degrade to Column::Str, breaking downstream cat-aware fast paths. Phase 6 adds an early-return at the top of eval_expr_column:

if let DExpr::Col(name) = expr {
    if let Some(src) = df.get_column(name) {
        match src {
            Column::Categorical { .. } | Column::CategoricalAdaptive(_) => {
                return Ok(src.clone());
            }
            _ => {}
        }
    }
}

Lazy optimizer: new ViewNode::StreamingGroupSummarise variant. New pass annotate_streamable_summarise rewrites GroupSummarise → StreamingGroupSummarise when every aggregation is streaming-friendly. All-or-nothing — any non-streamable agg keeps the legacy path.

Part 2 — The Deterministic Collection Family

Phase 7 onwards is a different track: not TidyView verbs but the collections those verbs use under the hood.

Phase 7 — Five deterministic collection types

A new module cjc_data::detcoll shipped:

Type Niche Lookup Iteration
IndexVec<I, V> dense IdType → Value O(1) insertion order
TinyDetMap<K, V> ≤ 16 entries linear scan sorted
SortedVecMap<K, V> small-medium sealed sorted binary search + range sorted
DetOpenMap<K, V> sparse mutable equality open addressing + BTreeMap fallback undefined-but-deterministic
DHarht<V> large sealed equality, byte-keys per-shard hash + microbucket undefined-but-deterministic

The first four are simple, but DHarht was meant to be the centerpiece — and the first version was slower than BTreeMap. The next four phases were about closing that gap.

Phase 8 — Slab + singleton + inline-keys SSO

Three constant-factor improvements:

  1. Per-shard typed slab allocator. Each Shard now owns a single key_pool: Vec<u8>. MicroBucket entries store KeyHandle { offset, len } instead of Vec<u8>. One allocation per shard instead of one per entry.
  2. Singleton front-entry fast path. First insertion at a prefix → FrontEntry::Singleton { handle, value } (no bucket allocation). Second insertion → promote.
  3. Inline-keys SSO. KeyHandle carries inline: [u8; 16]. Keys ≤ 16 bytes are stored inline; the slab is bypassed entirely on lookup.

Cumulative effect on a categorical-dictionary lookup bench (50k cats, 500k probes):

Stage DHarht/BTreeMap
Phase 7 baseline 0.34× (DHarht 3× slower)
Phase 8a (slab + singleton) 0.48×
Phase 8b (+ inline-keys SSO) 0.55×

Three rounds, ~62% improvement in ratio. Still slower than BTreeMap — the gap was clearly architectural, not micro-optimization.

Phase 9 — Hash swap + jump table

The categorical dict bench plateaued at 0.55× and I knew the hash function was burning cycles. Phase 9 swapped splitmix64+FNV per chunk for a tighter multiplicative mixer, then replaced the sorted-Vec front directory with a 256-slot jump table for O(1) slot lookup.

Result: marginal improvement on the categorical-dict bench. Still slower than BTreeMap on this workload. The bench shape was wrong.

Phase 10 — D-HARHT Memory profile (the real win)

Then I got hold of the actual D-HARHT blueprint the original architecture was based on, and the gap became obvious. My v.01 was a byte-key implementation; the original D-HARHT Memory profile is u64-keyed with very different choices:

Aspect D-HARHT Memory (their blueprint) DHarht v.01 (mine, Phase 7-9)
Key type u64 (no allocation) arbitrary &[u8] (slab + handles)
Front directory 16-bit, 65 536 slots, sparse-paged 8-bit, 256 slots, dense
Front entry Packed u64 with 3-bit tag Rust enum
MicroBucket 4 / 8 / 16 sized only 16
Match Parallel scalar match_mask Linear scan
Hash splitmix64 finalizer = scatter Multiplicative + finalize

Phase 10 ported the Memory profile faithfully as a sibling type DHarhtMemory<V>. Then I ran a 4-way bench on the same workload shape the blueprint used (100k u64 keys, 2M random-stream lookups, 3 stable runs):

Backend Run 1 Run 2 Run 3 Stable avg
BTreeMap 266 242 186 ~232 ns/op
HashMap (std SipHash) 148 91 84 ~107 ns/op
DHarht v.01 (byte-key) 238 185 217 ~213 ns/op
DHarht Memory (u64-key) 105 108 112 ~108 ns/op

DHarht Memory ≈ HashMap. And ~2.2× faster than BTreeMap. Architecture matters. The constant-factor optimizations in Phases 8 and 9 helped at the margins; the architectural shift from byte-key to u64-key + sparse paged 16-bit front directory was where the 2× win came from.

DHarht Memory’s run-to-run variance is also the smallest of any backend (±3%) — sealed lookup with cache-resident sparse pages is consistent in a way HashMap (with adaptive table sizing) is not.

Phase 11 — SealedU64Map wrapper + memory & security

Phase 10 shipped the architecture. Phase 11 packaged it for production use and ran the comparisons users care about beyond raw speed.

SealedU64Map<V> — a thin public wrapper around DHarhtMemory enforcing the build → seal → read-many lifecycle. Surface: new, insert, get, seal, iter_sorted, approx_memory_bytes, shape_hash. The recommended drop-in for any BTreeMap<u64, V> whose access pattern matches.

First wiring demo: ByteDictionary::seal_with_u64_hash_index() builds a u64-hash content-cache so callers who already have the deterministic 64-bit hash of bytes (from snapshot diffing, content-addressed storage) can look up the dictionary code via lookup_by_hash(hash) without rehashing.

Memory comparison (50k u64 entries, 8B keys + 8B values):

Backend Total bytes Bytes/entry vs HashMap
HashMap (std) 1 376 256 27.5 1.0×
BTreeMap 3 600 000 72.0 2.6×
DHarhtMemory 5 319 232 106.4 3.87×

HashMap wins on memory. DHarhtMemory pays ~3.87× more bytes for the 16-bit sparse-paged front directory + per-shard slabs + micro-bucket pools. That’s the cost of the determinism contract.

Determinism comparison:

Property BTreeMap HashMap DHarhtMemory
Same input → same iter order, same process ✅ ❌ randomized ✅
Same input → same iter order, across processes ✅ ❌ seed varies ✅
shape_hash byte-equal across builds ✅ n/a ✅

Empirically confirmed: HashMap iter order varied across 10 independent builds in the same process. This isn’t a bug — it’s HashDoS protection — but it’s exactly the property that disqualifies HashMap from canonical-output paths in CJC-Lang. DHarhtMemory’s shape_hash was 0xa7792345c13b05b0 stable across all builds.

Security comparison:

Threat BTreeMap HashMap DHarhtMemory
HashDoS adversarial collision ✅ Ord-tree immune ⚠️ random seed mitigates (loses determinism) ✅ MicroBucket16 cap → fallback
Silent entry loss ✅ ✅ ✅
False-positive lookups ✅ ✅ ✅ full key equality
Reproducibility for audit ✅ ❌ ✅
Adversarial diagnostics implicit none ✅ counters queryable

The trade is real and intentional: DHarhtMemory pays ~3.9× more memory than HashMap to deliver what HashMap structurally cannot — deterministic iteration across processes with HashDoS resistance. For a workspace whose hard rule is “same seed → bit-identical output across runs”, that’s exactly the profile that fits.

Phase 12 — ART fallback (port complete)

Phase 11 shipped DHarhtMemory using a BTreeMap<u64, V> as the per-table fallback when a front-directory collision group exceeded MicroBucket16. The original blueprint uses a per-shard Adaptive Radix Tree (ART) with the full Node4/Node16/Node32/Node48/Node256 hierarchy — I’d deferred that as a multi-day port.

Phase 12 lands it. Each shard now owns an ArtSlab<V> containing entries (3-bit-tagged u32 discriminators), leaves, and per-node-type arrays. When a collision group overflows during seal, those entries go into the originating shard’s ART rather than a global BTreeMap. Lookup with the FRONT_FALLBACK tag walks that shard’s ART by descending through bytes of the scattered key.

Adaptive growth is monotonic and deterministic: Node4 → Node16 → Node32 → Node48 → Node256 by child count, with the same insertion order producing byte-identical tree shape after compaction.

Verified by art_fallback_handles_forced_overflow: brute-search keys that collide on the same (shard, 16-bit prefix) until you have 17+ of them; verify that the seal triggers the ART path (micro_overflow_count > 0) and every key is still findable through the radix descent.

The win on collision-heavy workloads is structural — ART’s worst-case lookup is at most 8 byte-radix descents (one per byte of the 64-bit scatter) vs BTreeMap’s O(log N) tree walk. For the well-distributed workloads this engine targets, the ART path almost never fires; for adversarial inputs it caps the worst case deterministically.

Where this leaves CJC-Lang

The recommended backend selection table after v0.1.7:

Workload Pick
u64-keyed sealed lookup, deterministic + audit + reproducibility SealedU64Map
u64-keyed sealed lookup, lowest memory, no audit needs std::HashMap
u64-keyed mutable + ordered iteration BTreeMap
Arbitrary byte keys, sealed (categorical dictionaries, interning) DHarht v.01
Tiny tables (≤ 16 entries) TinyDetMap
Small-medium sealed sorted maps with range queries SortedVecMap
Sparse mutable equality, ordering doesn’t matter DetOpenMap
Dense IdType → Value ID tables IndexVec

The BTreeMap line is unchanged — it’s still the right answer for ordered iteration, range queries, and mutation-heavy workloads. The HashMap line is new: it’s faster than DHarhtMemory and uses less memory, but only if your workload doesn’t need reproducible iteration across runs. The vast majority of CJC-Lang’s data paths do — that’s why the language exists in the first place.

Numbers, in one place

Phase Bench Headline
2 group_by + cat keys 7.49× faster
3 Hybrid set ops 83.46× (favorable)
4 Categorical joins 6.08×
5(c) Categorical arrange 9.68×
6 Streaming summarise memory 25 000× less RAM
10 DHarht Memory u64 lookup matches HashMap (~108 ns/op)
11 Memory cost 3.87× HashMap
11 Determinism HashMap fails, DHarht passes
12 Adversarial collision ART fallback bounds worst case

Test counts (across the whole suite, end-to-end):

cjc-data         258  (was 193 in v0.1.6; +65 from v3 work)
test_phase10_tidy 495  (was 379 in v0.1.6; +116 from v3 work)
bolero fuzz       32   (was 26 in v0.1.6; +6)
tidyview-bench    38   (unchanged)
physics_ml        71   (unchanged)

Workspace builds clean. Vault audit: 1 738 / 1 738 wikilinks resolve. cjc-lang v0.1.7 packages cleanly via cargo publish --dry-run.

What’s deferred (with reasons)

Honest scope notes — these landed on the future-work list rather than v0.1.7:

  • Full Column::Categorical → CategoricalColumn storage replacement. The additive CategoricalAdaptive variant in Phase 5(a) is the gateway; the literal storage swap touches ~200 column-reader call sites. Single-session work won’t do it justice.
  • LookupProfile::Speed and LookupProfile::Balanced. The Memory profile is the recommended starting point per the original blueprint. Speed adds ~6 MB per shard for a marginal lookup-time improvement; not worth it until measured demand appears.
  • Manual SSE2 find_key16_sse2 for ART nodes. The scalar match_mask already inlines into tight code; SIMD here would be unsafe + architecture-specific for a path that fires only on adversarial input.
  • Full compiler/runtime wiring of SealedU64Map beyond the ByteDictionary content-hash demo. Symbol interning, MIR value tables, runtime globals — each is its own integration point and needs its own measurement.

Try it

cargo install cjc-lang  # picks up v0.1.7 once published

Or pull the workspace and run the bench yourself:

git clone https://github.com/AdamEzzat1/CJC
cd CJC
cargo test --test test_phase10_tidy --release -- --ignored bench_phase10 --nocapture

The 4-way bench output should look like the table above. If yours is different — say, HashMap is much faster than mine — that’s interesting data. The blueprint reported ~37 ns/op for both DHarht Memory and HashMap on the original author’s hardware; I see ~108 ns/op vs ~107 ns/op on Windows. Same ratio, different absolute. Architecture is portable; constant factors are not.

If you spot a workload where the chosen backend is wrong for the shape, that’s exactly the kind of feedback that drove this release. The collection family is open-ended — Phase 13 will be whatever shape isn’t covered yet.

item.title: The cjcl CLI: 30 Commands for Deterministic Computing
item.dc:creator: Adam Ezzat
item.description:
🕑 ~20 min read
30commands
4categories
0external deps
42default seed
TL;DR

cjcl is a single binary with 30 commands. Install with cargo install cjc-lang, then cjcl run your_file.cjcl. Every command produces deterministic output — same input, same flags, same result, every time.

What Is cjcl?

cjcl is the CLI frontend for CJC-Lang, a deterministic numerical programming language. One binary, zero external dependencies, 30 commands that span compilation, data engineering, profiling, and reproducibility verification.

cargo install cjc-lang
cjcl --version   # CJC-Lang v0.1.6

Every command that produces output supports three output modes: --color (default, human-readable), --plain (no ANSI escapes, pipe-friendly), and --json (machine-readable). Most commands that execute code accept --seed <N> for deterministic RNG seeding (default: 42).

Quick Reference

Category Commands
Core Compiler lex, parse, check, run, eval, repl
Data & Pipeline view, proof, flow, patch, seek, drift, forge
Inspection & Diagnostics inspect, schema, trace, mem, bench, pack, doctor
Compiler Visibility emit, explain, gc, nogc, audit, precision, lock, parity, test, ci

Core Compiler Commands

These six commands are the foundation — tokenize, parse, type-check, run, evaluate, and interact.

lex

Tokenize a source file and print the token stream.

cjcl lex program.cjcl

Output shows each token’s span, kind, and text. Useful for debugging parser issues or understanding how CJC-Lang sees your source code.

parse

Parse a source file and pretty-print the AST.

cjcl parse program.cjcl

Shows the full abstract syntax tree after parsing. Use this when you suspect a precedence or grouping issue — the AST reveals exactly how the parser interpreted your expressions.

check

Type-check a source file without running it.

cjcl check program.cjcl

Runs the full compilation pipeline (lex, parse, type-check) and reports errors and warnings. Exit code 0 means the program is well-typed.

When used with validation flags, check becomes an output validator:

cjcl check output.csv --against expected.csv --tolerance 1e-6
cjcl check output.csv --against expected.csv --schema-only
cjcl check output.txt --expect "Hello, world!"
Flag What it does
--against <file> Compare file against a reference
--tolerance <N> Numeric tolerance for comparison (default: 0.0)
--schema-only Compare only column structure, not values
--expect <string> Check file contents match a literal string

When to use it: Run cjcl check before committing to catch type errors early. Use the validation mode in CI to verify pipeline outputs haven’t drifted.

run

Execute a CJC-Lang program.

cjcl run program.cjcl
cjcl run program.cjcl --seed 123
cjcl run program.cjcl --mir-opt
cjcl run program.cjcl --multi-file

This is the command you’ll use most. By default it uses the AST tree-walk interpreter. Add --mir-opt to use the MIR register-machine executor with optimizations (control flow simplification + dead code elimination).

Flag What it does
--seed <N> RNG seed (default: 42)
--mir-opt MIR executor with optimizations
--mir-mono MIR executor with monomorphization
--multi-file Enable multi-file module resolution
--time Print execution time to stderr
--format json Wrap output in {"ok":true,"output":[...]}

Exit codes: 0 = success, 1 = runtime error, 2 = parse error, 3 = type error, 4 = parity failure.

When to use it: This is your daily driver. Use --mir-opt for performance-sensitive code, --multi-file for projects split across modules.

eval

Evaluate a single expression without creating a file.

cjcl eval "42 + 8"           # → 50
cjcl eval "sqrt(2.0)"        # → 1.4142135623730951
cjcl eval "42 + 8" --format json  # → {"ok":true,"output":["50"]}

The expression is wrapped in fn main() { print(<expr>); } and executed. Supports --seed and --format.

When to use it: Quick calculations, testing builtins, scripting one-liners.

repl

Start an interactive read-eval-print loop.

cjcl repl
cjcl repl --seed 123

The REPL preserves variable bindings across lines and supports meta-commands:

Command What it does
:help Show all meta-commands
:type <expr> Show inferred type
:ast <expr> Pretty-print the AST
:mir <expr> Show MIR representation
:env Show current variable bindings
:time <expr> Time expression execution
:describe <expr> Statistical summary of a numeric array
:save <file> Save session history
:load <file> Load and execute a file
:reset Clear all bindings
:seed Show current RNG seed
:quit Exit

When to use it: Exploratory data analysis, testing ideas, learning the language.

Data & Pipeline Commands

Seven commands for working with data files, verifying reproducibility, and building deterministic pipelines.

view

Deterministic directory listing with type-aware annotations.

cjcl view
cjcl view ./data/ --recursive --hash

Lists files sorted lexicographically with color-coded types: .cjcl files show effect annotations (#[nogc], @pure, @timed), .snap files show content hashes, data files (.csv, .json) are highlighted. Output is deterministic — same directory, same listing, every time.

Flag What it does
-r, --recursive Recurse into subdirectories
--hash Show SHA-256 hashes
--no-effects Hide effect annotations
--no-size Hide file sizes

When to use it: Quickly inventory a project’s data files and verify effect annotations.

proof

Verify that a program produces identical output across multiple runs.

cjcl proof program.cjcl
cjcl proof program.cjcl --runs 10 --seeds 42,123,999
cjcl proof program.cjcl --executor both --hash-output

Runs the program N times (default: 3) and checks that stdout, exit status, and GC behavior are identical. With --seeds, tests multiple seeds. With --executor both, verifies both the AST interpreter and MIR executor.

Flag What it does
-n, --runs <N> Number of iterations (default: 3)
--seed <N> Single seed to test (default: 42)
--seeds <N,M,...> Test multiple seeds
--executor eval\|mir\|both Which executor(s) to use
--fail-fast Stop on first divergence
--hash-output Show SHA-256 of stdout per run
--save-report <file> Save report as JSON

When to use it: Before publishing results. Before merging a PR that touches numerical code. In CI as a gate.

flow

Streaming computation over CSV/TSV/JSONL data with O(ncols) memory.

cjcl flow sales.csv --op sum,mean,min,max
cjcl flow sales.csv --op mean --columns revenue,cost --sort-by mean
cjcl flow --tsv < data.tsv --top 5 --json

Processes data in a single pass without loading the entire file into memory. Uses Kahan summation for numeric stability and Welford’s online algorithm for variance/stddev.

Flag What it does
--op <ops> Operations: sum, mean, min, max, count, var, std
-c, --columns <cols> Only process named columns
--sort-by <metric> Sort output by a metric
--top <N> Show only top N columns
--verify Run twice, confirm identical output
--tsv Tab-delimited input
-d, --delimiter <c> Custom delimiter

When to use it: Quick summary statistics on large CSVs without loading into a DataFrame. Verifying that a pipeline’s output statistics match expectations.

patch

Type-aware data transformation for CSV/TSV/JSONL files.

cjcl patch data.csv --nan-fill 0.0
cjcl patch data.csv --impute salary --drop temp_col --rename old_name new_name
cjcl patch data.csv --replace status "NA" "unknown" --plan

Streams the file and applies transformations: NaN replacement, mean imputation, column dropping, renaming, value replacement. With --plan, shows the transformation sequence without executing. With --dry-run, previews changes.

Flag What it does
--nan-fill <value> Replace NaN/NA/null with a value
--impute <column> Mean imputation for a column
--drop <column> Drop a column
--rename <from> <to> Rename a column
--replace <col> <from> <to> Replace exact values
--fill-empty <col> <value> Fill empty cells
--plan Show transformation plan
--dry-run Preview without writing
--in-place Modify file directly
-o, --output <file> Output file (default: stdout)

When to use it: Quick data cleaning before feeding into a CJC-Lang pipeline. Fixing nulls and renaming columns without writing a script.

seek

Deterministic file discovery with content search.

cjcl seek . "*.cjcl"
cjcl seek ./data --type csv --contains "revenue" --hash
cjcl seek . --min-size 1MB --sort size --manifest

Recursively finds files matching patterns with stable, sorted output. With --contains, searches file contents. With --manifest, outputs <hash> <size> <path> per line — useful for reproducibility manifests.

Flag What it does
-t, --type <type> Filter by extension (cjcl, csv, snap, etc.)
--contains <pattern> Content search
--min-size <size> Minimum file size (supports KB/MB)
--max-size <size> Maximum file size
--max-depth <N> Recursion depth limit
--hash Show SHA-256 per file
--manifest Output hash, size, path per line
--sort name\|size\|modified Sort mode
--first <N> Limit results
--ignore-build-artifacts Skip target/, node_modules/, etc.

When to use it: Building reproducibility manifests. Finding all data files in a project. Content search across a codebase.

drift

Compare two files with mathematical precision.

cjcl drift output_v1.csv output_v2.csv --tolerance 1e-6
cjcl drift model_a.csv model_b.csv --stats-only --json
cjcl drift old.txt new.txt --text --fail-on-diff

Supports text (line-by-line), numeric (element-wise), CSV (cell-by-cell), and JSONL diff modes. Reports max deviation, mean deviation, Frobenius norm, and per-cell differences.

Flag What it does
--tolerance <N> Numeric tolerance (default: 0.0)
--text, --numeric, --csv, --jsonl Diff mode (auto-detected from extension)
--max-diffs <N> Maximum differences to show (default: 50)
--fail-on-diff Exit 1 if any differences found
--fail-on-schema-diff Exit 1 if column names differ
--summary-only Only show summary metrics
--stats-only Only show statistical metrics
--report <file> Write full diff report to JSON

When to use it: Comparing model outputs between versions. Verifying that a code change didn’t alter numerical results. Regression testing in CI.

forge

Content-addressable pipeline runner.

cjcl forge run pipeline.cjcl
cjcl forge verify pipeline.cjcl abc123def
cjcl forge list
cjcl forge show abc123
cjcl forge clean

Executes a program, captures its output, and stores the result as a SHA-256 content-addressable artifact. With verify, checks that the current output matches an expected hash. Artifacts are stored in .cjc-forge/.

Subcommand What it does
run <file> Execute and store artifact
verify <file> <hash> Verify output matches hash
list List cached artifacts
show <hash-prefix> Show artifact details
clean Remove all cached artifacts
Flag What it does
--seed <N> RNG seed (default: 42)
--cache-dir <path> Cache directory (default: .cjc-forge/)

When to use it: Pinning pipeline outputs for reproducibility. Verifying that a computation produces the exact same output on a different machine.

Inspection & Diagnostics

Seven commands for understanding what your code does, how fast it runs, and whether your project is healthy.

inspect

Deep inspection of structured files without execution.

cjcl inspect data.csv
cjcl inspect model.snap --deep
cjcl inspect results.csv --compare expected.csv
cjcl inspect data.parquet --schema-only

Inspects .snap, CSV/TSV, JSONL, .cjcl, Parquet, Arrow, SQLite, and model files. Reports structure, shape, column types, null counts, and basic statistics. With --deep, computes variance, standard deviation, and unique value counts.

Flag What it does
--deep Compute variance, unique values
--preview <N> Max preview lines (default: 5)
--header-only Show header/schema only
--compare <file> Compare schemas/hashes with another file
--hash Show SHA-256 hash
--manifest Output manifest format

When to use it: Understanding unfamiliar datasets. Verifying schema before piping into a pipeline. Quick sanity checks on model checkpoints.

schema

Infer and validate data schemas.

cjcl schema data.csv
cjcl schema data.csv --full --save schema.json
cjcl schema data.csv --check schema.json
cjcl schema data.csv --diff old_schema.json

Infers column types (null, bool, int, float, string, mixed), null counts, and unique value counts from CSV/TSV/JSONL/Parquet/Arrow/SQLite data. With --save, persists the schema. With --check, validates data against a saved schema. With --diff, shows schema drift.

Flag What it does
--full Show type distributions and sample values
--save <file> Save schema to JSON
--check <file> Validate against saved schema (exit 1 on mismatch)
--diff <file> Compare current vs saved schema
--sample <N> Limit to first N rows
--strict Treat warnings as errors

When to use it: Detecting schema drift between pipeline runs. Validating incoming data before processing. Documenting dataset structure.

trace

Execution tracing across four phases: parse, static analysis, execution, and reporting.

cjcl trace program.cjcl
cjcl trace program.cjcl --ast --verbose

Reports parse time, execution time, AST structure (function/struct/statement counts), effect annotations, and runtime behavior (output lines, GC collections).

Flag What it does
--ast Show AST details
--no-output Suppress program output
-v, --verbose Show function list

When to use it: Understanding the structure of a program. Getting a quick overview of parse and execution cost.
