mem

Runtime memory profiling.

cjcl mem program.cjcl
cjcl mem program.cjcl --runs 5 --timeline --compare eval|mir

Executes a program and reports GC collection counts, heap object counts, output size, and execution time. With --runs, aggregates statistics across multiple executions. With --compare, runs both executors and shows side-by-side GC behavior.

Flag What it does
-n, --runs <N> Number of runs (default: 1)
--nogc Run NoGC verification first
--eval / --mir Choose executor
--peak-only Only report peak metrics
--timeline Show GC collections per run
--compare eval\|mir Compare executor GC behavior
--fail-on-gc Exit 1 if any GC collections occurred
--save-report <file> Save report as JSON

When to use it: Identifying GC pressure. Verifying that a #[nogc] function truly avoids allocations. Comparing memory behavior between executors.

bench

Performance benchmarking with statistical rigor.

cjcl bench program.cjcl --runs 20 --warmup 5
cjcl bench program.cjcl --compare eval|mir --markdown
cjcl bench program.cjcl --baseline baseline.json --fail-if-slower-than 10

Runs the program N times with warmup iterations and reports mean, median, min, max, stddev, coefficient of variation, P95, and throughput.

Flag What it does
-n, --runs <N> Measured runs (default: 10)
--warmup <N> Warmup runs (default: 1)
--mir / --eval Choose executor
--compare eval\|mir Side-by-side executor comparison
--baseline <file> Load baseline for comparison
--save-baseline <file> Save results as baseline
--fail-if-slower-than <N> Exit 1 if >N% slower than baseline
--csv / --markdown Output as CSV or Markdown table
-v, --verbose Show per-run times

When to use it: Measuring performance before and after a change. Comparing executor backends. Setting up CI performance gates.

pack

Bundle a program and its dependencies into a reproducible package.

cjcl pack pipeline.cjcl
cjcl pack pipeline.cjcl --include extra_data.csv --verify
cjcl pack --list pipeline.pack
cjcl pack --repro-check pipeline.pack

Auto-discovers dependencies by scanning source for string literals matching packable extensions (.snap, .csv, .tsv, .json, .lock, .toml). Creates a directory with a MANIFEST containing per-file SHA-256 hashes.

Flag What it does
-o, --output <dir> Output directory
--include <file> Additional file to include (repeatable)
--verify Verify manifest after packing
--dry-run Preview without writing
--list <dir.pack> List package contents
--repro-check <dir> Re-hash and verify against manifest
--manifest-only Output MANIFEST only

When to use it: Sharing reproducible computations. Archiving pipeline artifacts. Verifying that a package hasn’t been tampered with.

doctor

Project-wide diagnostics.

cjcl doctor
cjcl doctor ./my_project --fix --dry-run
cjcl doctor --strict --report findings.json

Scans a directory for: parse errors, type errors, corrupt .snap files, ragged CSV rows, nondeterministic patterns (HashMap, HashSet, rand(), time()), large files, malformed JSONL, and invalid Parquet files.

Flag What it does
--fix Attempt automatic fixes
--dry-run Preview fixes without applying
--strict Exit 1 on warnings
--category <type> Filter by finding category
--report <file> Save findings to JSON
--summary-only Only show summary counts
--fail-on <level> Exit 1 at severity threshold

When to use it: Pre-commit health check. CI gate for project hygiene. Onboarding — run doctor to understand a new project’s state.

Compiler Visibility & Analysis

Ten commands that expose the compiler’s internals — intermediate representations, memory verification, numerical analysis, and dual-executor parity.

emit

Dump intermediate representations at any pipeline stage.

cjcl emit program.cjcl --stage ast
cjcl emit program.cjcl --stage hir
cjcl emit program.cjcl --stage mir
cjcl emit program.cjcl --stage mir --opt --diff
Flag What it does
-s, --stage ast\|hir\|mir Pipeline stage (default: mir)
--opt Show optimized MIR
--diff Show both unoptimized and optimized MIR

When to use it: Understanding what the compiler does to your code. Debugging unexpected behavior by inspecting the IR. Verifying that optimizations preserve semantics.

explain

Show the desugared form of a program after HIR lowering.

cjcl explain program.cjcl
cjcl explain program.cjcl --verbose

Shows function signatures, effect annotations, struct definitions, and a summary table. With --verbose, shows full HIR bodies. Useful for understanding what syntactic sugar (for-loops, match expressions, closures) compiles down to.

gc

Dedicated GC analysis with allocation timeline.

cjcl gc program.cjcl --runs 5 --verbose

Executes the program multiple times and reports GC pause count, collections per run, heap object peaks, COW copies, and execution time. Shows whether GC behavior is stable across runs.

Flag What it does
-n, --runs <N> Number of runs (default: 3)
-v, --verbose Show allocation timeline

When to use it: Identifying allocation hotspots. Verifying GC stability for determinism.

nogc

Static verification that #[nogc] functions don’t allocate.

cjcl nogc program.cjcl
cjcl nogc program.cjcl --verbose

Lists all functions with their #[nogc] annotation status and runs the NoGC verifier. Reports per-function PASS/FAIL with violation details. Exit code 1 if any violations found.

When to use it: Verifying that performance-critical functions avoid GC. Required for functions that will run in the NoGC execution mode.

audit

Static analysis for floating-point anti-patterns.

cjcl audit program.cjcl
cjcl audit program.cjcl --strict --category summation
cjcl audit program.cjcl --baseline baseline.json --report findings.json

Detects: naive summation in loops (suggests kahan_sum()), float equality comparisons (suggests approx_eq()), unguarded division, and potential catastrophic cancellation. No execution required — purely static analysis.

Flag What it does
--strict Treat INFO as warnings, warnings as errors
--category <type> Filter: summation, float-eq, division, cancellation
--baseline <file> Only report new findings
--report <file> Save findings as JSON
--suggest-only Only show suggestions

When to use it: Code review for numerical code. CI gate for floating-point hygiene. Onboarding — audit unfamiliar numerical code before trusting its output.

precision

Analyze whether f64 precision matters for your output.

cjcl precision program.cjcl
cjcl precision program.cjcl --epsilon 1e-10 --fail-on-instability

Executes the program, extracts numeric values from output, compares f64 against f32-truncated values, and reports relative error per value. Verdict: STABLE (within epsilon) or SENSITIVE (exceeds epsilon).

Flag What it does
--epsilon <N> Comparison threshold (default: ~1.19e-7, f32 epsilon)
--fail-on-instability Exit 1 if any value exceeds epsilon
--summary-only Only show pass/fail
--report <file> Save report as JSON

When to use it: Deciding whether f32 is sufficient for a workload. Identifying precision-sensitive computations. CI gate for numerical stability.

lock

Generate and verify deterministic lockfiles.

cjcl lock program.cjcl
cjcl lock program.cjcl --verify
cjcl lock program.cjcl --show
cjcl lock program.cjcl --diff
cjcl lock program.cjcl --executor mir --update

Creates a lockfile containing: source SHA-256 hash, seed, executor version, platform, and expected output SHA-256 hash. With --verify, checks that the current execution matches the recorded lockfile.

Flag What it does
--verify Verify current run against lockfile
--update Regenerate lockfile
--show Display lockfile contents
--diff Show field-by-field differences
--executor eval\|mir Which executor (default: eval)

When to use it: Pinning expected output for CI. Detecting when a code or platform change alters results. Cross-platform reproducibility verification.

parity

Verify that both executors produce identical results.

cjcl parity program.cjcl
cjcl parity program.cjcl --verbose --explain-mismatch
cjcl parity program.cjcl --function my_function --save-report parity.json

Runs both the AST interpreter (v1) and MIR executor (v2) with the same seed and compares stdout, exit status, return value, and GC collections.

Flag What it does
-v, --verbose Show output from both executors
--explain-mismatch Show first divergent line with context
--function <name> Only test a specific function
--save-report <file> Save report as JSON

When to use it: After implementing a new language feature. Before shipping a compiler change. CI gate for executor equivalence.

test

Native test runner for CJC-Lang test functions.

cjcl test program.cjcl
cjcl test program.cjcl --filter "matrix"
cjcl test program.cjcl --verbose

Discovers functions whose name starts with test_ (or decorated with @test), runs them in alphabetical order with a fresh interpreter per test, and reports pass/fail with timing.

Flag What it does
--filter <pattern> Only run matching tests
-v, --verbose Show test output and errors

When to use it: Running tests written in CJC-Lang itself. Quick smoke tests without a Rust test harness.

ci

Full CI diagnostic suite — runs five checks in sequence.

cjcl ci
cjcl ci ./my_project --strict --json

Orchestrates: doctor (parse/type errors) → proof (determinism) → parity (dual-executor) → test (test functions) → nogc (static verification). Reports per-step pass/fail.

Flag What it does
--seed <N> Base RNG seed (default: 42)
--strict Exit 1 on warnings

When to use it: One command in your CI pipeline that checks everything. The nuclear option for project health.

Putting It Together

The real power of the CLI is in composition. Here are four workflows I use regularly:

“I just wrote new code”

cjcl check program.cjcl          # Type errors?
cjcl run program.cjcl             # Does it work?
cjcl parity program.cjcl          # Same result on both executors?

“My pipeline is slow”

cjcl trace program.cjcl           # Where does time go?
cjcl mem program.cjcl --timeline  # GC pressure?
cjcl bench program.cjcl --runs 20 --compare eval|mir  # Which executor is faster?

“I’m shipping a reproducible artifact”

cjcl lock program.cjcl            # Pin expected output
cjcl pack program.cjcl --verify   # Bundle with manifest
cjcl proof program.cjcl --runs 10 --seeds 42,123,999  # Determinism across seeds

“The numbers don’t look right”

cjcl audit program.cjcl           # Float anti-patterns?
cjcl precision program.cjcl       # f64 vs f32 sensitivity?
cjcl drift output_v1.csv output_v2.csv --tolerance 1e-6  # What changed?

Global Flags

These flags work with any command:

Flag What it does
--seed <N> RNG seed (default: 42)
--reproducible Enable reproducibility mode
--time Print execution time to stderr
--color / --no-color Control ANSI color output
--diagnostic-format rich\|short Error display format
--help, -h Print help
--version, -V Print version

CJC-Lang is open source and available on crates.io. All 30 commands are included in the single cjcl binary. Run cjcl <command> --help for command-specific help.

item.title: TidyView: A Database Engine in Disguise
item.dc:creator: Adam Ezzat
item.description:
🕑 ~30 min read
3layers
103operations
0HashMaps
38benchmark tests
TL;DR

TidyView is CJC-Lang’s data manipulation system. It looks like R’s dplyr on the surface, but underneath it’s a database engine: columnar storage, lazy evaluation, bitmask filtering, predicate pushdown, and zone maps. The result is a data pipeline where every intermediate state is inspectable, the original data is never mutated, and the same input always produces the same output — down to the last bit, on every run. (Tested on Windows; cross-platform verification is ongoing.)

The Problem

When I started building CJC-Lang, the core promise was simple: same seed, same output, always. Every part of the language enforces this — ordered collections, Kahan summation, seeded RNG, no FMA in SIMD kernels.

But then I needed to handle data. Filter a table. Group rows. Compute aggregates. Join two datasets.

The obvious move was to reach for an existing DataFrame library. But when I looked at what’s available, I kept running into the same problems:

Hash-based grouping. Most DataFrame libraries — including Pandas and Polars — use hash maps to group rows. Hash maps are fast, but their iteration order may depend on internal hash seeds or execution strategy. Depending on the configuration, grouping the same data twice can produce rows in a different order. That makes bit-identical reproducibility difficult to guarantee.

Floating-point reduction order. Call sum() on a million floats in Pandas. The result depends on the order of accumulation. Add the numbers in a different order and you get different rounding errors. Small differences, but they compound — and they mean “same data, different result” depending on implementation details. (Polars and other libraries may use more sophisticated reduction strategies, but the result can still vary across execution modes or platforms.)

Parallel scheduling. Libraries that use thread pools for parallel execution (Polars uses Rayon, for example) gain significant throughput. But thread scheduling is inherently non-deterministic, which means parallel aggregation can introduce ordering variation between runs — even with identical input. Whether this matters depends on the workload, but for bit-identical reproducibility it’s a concern.

Aliasing mutations. Pandas has an entire warning category (SettingWithCopyWarning) dedicated to the fact that modifying a filtered subset might or might not modify the original DataFrame. Whether it does depends on internal memory layout decisions that are invisible to the user.

None of these are bugs. They’re design trade-offs made by libraries that prioritize speed over reproducibility. But they break CJC-Lang’s core contract. So I built something else.

What TidyView Is

TidyView is a three-layer data manipulation system:

Layer Type What it does
Immutable view TidyView Zero-copy lens: bitmask + projection over shared data
Materialized frame TidyFrame Copy-on-write buffer for mutations
Grouped view GroupedTidyView Group index for aggregation

The API surface is modeled on R’s dplyr — filter, select, mutate, group_by, summarise, arrange, join. If you’ve used the tidyverse, you already know the vocabulary.

But the implementation borrows from a different tradition entirely: analytical databases.

How Data Flows Through a TidyView Pipeline

flowchart TD
    A["CSV / Data Source"] --> B["Base DataFrame<br/><i>immutable, Rc-shared</i>"]
    B --> C["TidyView<br/><i>zero-copy lens</i>"]
    C --> D["filter( )<br/><i>bitmask A — no data copied</i>"]
    D --> E["filter( )<br/><i>bitmask B — AND with A</i>"]
    E --> F["group_by( )<br/><i>BTreeMap index over visible rows</i>"]
    F --> G["summarise( )<br/><i>Kahan reduction per group</i>"]
    G --> H["Materialized Result<br/><i>TidyFrame — new data</i>"]

    style A fill:#f9f9f9,stroke:#333
    style B fill:#e8f4fd,stroke:#2196F3,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#e8f4fd,stroke:#2196F3
    style D fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#FF9800
    style E fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#FF9800
    style F fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#9C27B0
    style G fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#9C27B0
    style H fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#4CAF50,stroke-width:2px

Every step from the base DataFrame through the filters shares the same underlying data. New memory is only allocated for bitmasks (orange) and the final materialized result (green). The original data (blue) is never modified.

The Database Connection

I didn’t set out to build a database engine. But the constraints I was working under — determinism, auditability, memory safety — kept pushing me toward the same solutions that database engineers arrived at decades ago.

Columnar Storage

Data is stored column-major:

// Internally:
columns: [
    ("name",    Str(["Alice", "Bob", "Carol", "Dave"])),
    ("age",     Int([30, 25, 35, 28])),
    ("revenue", Float([150.0, 200.0, 175.0, 225.0])),
]

Each column is a contiguous typed buffer. When you compute sum(revenue), the runtime scans a single Vec<f64> — no pointer chasing, no skipping over unrelated fields. This is the same storage model used by Apache Arrow, DuckDB, and Parquet.

The alternative — row-major storage — would scatter each revenue value across a row struct alongside name and age data. For a table with 100 columns, computing a single-column aggregate would touch 100x more memory than necessary.

Bitmask Filtering

This is the design choice that makes everything else work. When you filter a TidyView, no data is copied. Instead, a bitmask records which rows passed the predicate:

// Original data: 4 rows
base: ["Alice", "Bob", "Carol", "Dave"]  // ages: 30, 25, 35, 28

// After filter(age > 28):
mask: [1, 0, 1, 1]  // Alice (30), Carol (35), Dave (28 fails, hypothetical: > 28)

In practice the mask is packed into 64-bit words — one bit per row. A million-row filter costs 122 KB of bitmask memory. The original data stays exactly where it was, untouched.

Chain three filters together? The bitmasks are ANDed. Still no data copied. Still 122 KB.

let sales: Any = read_csv("sales.csv");
let df: Any = sales
    |> filter(col("region") == "West")
    |> filter(col("revenue") > 1000.0)
    |> filter(col("year") == 2025);

Each filter call returns a new TidyView with a new bitmask. All three views share the same underlying data. The CSV is loaded once. This is the same technique used by bitmap indexes in Oracle and selection vectors in DuckDB.

Predicate Pushdown

TidyView has a lazy evaluation layer where operations build a logical plan tree before executing. The optimizer rewrites this tree using three classic database passes:

Pass 1 — Filter merging. Consecutive filters are combined into a single filter with an AND predicate.

Pass 2 — Predicate pushdown. Filters are pushed as close to the data source as possible.

Pass 3 — Redundant select elimination. Select nodes that project all columns are removed.

Here’s why this matters. Suppose you write:

let result: Any = df
    |> mutate("bonus", col("salary") * 0.1)
    |> filter(col("department") == "Engineering");

Without optimization, this computes bonus for every row, then filters. With predicate pushdown, the optimizer recognizes that department == "Engineering" doesn’t depend on the bonus column, so it pushes the filter below the mutate. Now filtering happens first, and bonus is only computed for engineering rows.

The pushdown rules follow standard relational algebra:

  • Push past select — always safe
  • Push past arrange — filtering doesn’t affect sort order
  • Push past mutate — only if the predicate doesn’t reference a mutated column
  • Push into join branches — if the predicate references only one side
  • Never past group_by + summarise — aggregation changes row identity
  • Never past distinct — uniqueness changes row identity

These are the same rules taught in database systems textbooks. They apply here because TidyView’s operation set is relational algebra — just with tidyverse names.

Zone Maps

Each column tracks lightweight statistics:

// ColumnStats for the "revenue" column:
min: 150.0
max: 225.0
sorted_ascending: false
n_distinct: 4

When you write filter(col("revenue") > 500.0), the zone map says: max is 225, so no row can satisfy > 500. The entire filter can be skipped.

When a column is sorted, the system uses binary search instead of a linear scan — O(log N) instead of O(N).

These are the same optimizations that Vertica, Redshift, and Parquet use at the storage layer. In TidyView they operate at the column level because the entire “database” fits in memory.

What This Prevents

The architectural choices above aren’t just about performance. They prevent specific classes of bugs that plague traditional DataFrame workflows.

The Aliasing Problem

In Pandas:

df = pd.DataFrame({"x": [1, 2, 3], "y": [4, 5, 6]})
subset = df[df.x > 1]
subset["z"] = 99      # Does this modify df? Maybe.

Whether subset["z"] = 99 modifies the original df depends on whether Pandas chose to return a view or a copy when you wrote df[df.x > 1]. This is the SettingWithCopyWarning — one of the most common sources of confusion in data science code.

In TidyView, this problem doesn’t exist. The base data lives behind an Rc (reference-counted pointer) and is never modified. When you need to mutate, TidyFrame checks the reference count:

  • If only one reference exists, mutate in place
  • If multiple references exist, deep-clone first, then mutate the clone

The check is deterministic — it’s a simple integer comparison, not a heuristic. The other views are never affected.

The Order-Dependent Mutation Problem

In Pandas:

df = df.assign(a = df.x + 1, b = df.a * 2)

Does df.a in the second expression see the newly computed a or the original a? In Pandas, it sees the new one. The result depends on the order of the assignments. Swap a and b and you get different data.

In TidyView, mutate uses snapshot semantics: all column references are validated against the column list frozen before any assignments execute. Every expression sees the data as it was when the mutate call started:

let result: Any = df
    |> mutate("a", col("x") + 1)
    |> mutate("b", col("a") * 2);
// col("a") always reads the ORIGINAL "a" column
// If "a" didn't exist before, this is an error — not a silent dependency

This is the same principle as SQL’s UPDATE SET a = b, b = a — the SET clause reads from a snapshot, so this correctly swaps the values rather than setting both to b.

The Hidden Null Problem

Pandas DataFrames can have NaN or None in any column at any time. You discover this at runtime, usually when an aggregation silently skips missing values and gives you a number that’s subtly wrong.

In TidyView, the base Column type has no null representation:

// Column — always fully populated:
Int(Vec<i64>)
Float(Vec<f64>)
Str(Vec<String>)
Bool(Vec<bool>)

There’s no Option wrapper. No sentinel values. If you have a TidyView, its data is guaranteed null-free by type.

Nulls exist, but they’re quarantined in a separate type called NullCol. You only encounter NullCol through operations that can introduce nulls — left joins (unmatched rows), pivot_wider (missing combinations), full joins. These operations return a NullableFrame instead of a TidyFrame, and the type boundary makes the null-possibility explicit.

The question “does my data have nulls?” is answered by the type, not by scanning every cell.

The Floating-Point Drift Problem

# Pandas
pd.Series([1e16, 1.0, -1e16]).sum()  # → 0.0 (wrong)

The mathematically correct answer is 1.0. Pandas gives 0.0 because it uses naive left-to-right summation: 1e16 + 1.0 rounds to 1e16 (the 1.0 is lost to floating-point precision), then 1e16 - 1e16 = 0.0.

TidyView uses Kahan compensated summation for every floating-point reduction — sum, mean, variance, sd, all of them. Kahan summation tracks a correction term that captures the rounding error:

// Kahan summation:
//   sum = 0, c = 0
//   y = 1e16 - 0 = 1e16;    t = 0 + 1e16 = 1e16;    c = (1e16 - 1e16) - 1e16 = 0
//   y = 1.0 - 0 = 1.0;      t = 1e16 + 1.0 = 1e16;   c = (1e16 - 1e16) - 1.0 = -1.0
//   y = -1e16 - (-1.0);      ...compensates the lost 1.0
//   Result: 1.0 (correct)

Same input, same result, every time. Not approximately correct — exactly correct within the limits of double precision.

TidyView in Practice

The architecture above is interesting in the abstract, but what does it actually look like when you write code? Here’s a walkthrough of real CJC-Lang programs using TidyView, showing how operations chain together and how you can audit every step.

Loading and Filtering

let csv = "name,age,city\nAlice,30,NYC\nBob,25,LA\nCharlie,35,NYC\nDiana,28,LA\n";
let df = Csv.parse(csv);
let view = df.view();

// filter: age > 28 (keeps Alice=30, Charlie=35)
let older = view.filter(dexpr_binop(">", col("age"), 28));
print(older.nrows());   // → 2

Csv.parse() returns a DataFrame. .view() wraps it in a TidyView — a zero-copy lens over the data. The filter call doesn’t copy any rows. It creates a new bitmask: [1, 0, 1, 0]. The original four rows are still sitting in the same memory, untouched.

Chaining Operations

TidyView operations return new views or frames, so they chain naturally. Each step narrows, reshapes, or aggregates the data — and every intermediate result is a value you can bind to a variable and inspect later.

let csv = "dept,name,salary\nEng,Alice,100\nEng,Bob,120\nSales,Carol,80\nSales,Dave,90\nEng,Eve,110\n";
let df = Csv.parse(csv);
let view = df.view();

// Chain: filter → group → summarise
let result = view
    .filter(dexpr_binop(">", col("salary"), 85))
    .group_by(["dept"])
    .summarise("n", tidy_count(), "avg", tidy_mean("salary"));
print(result.nrows());   // → 2 (Eng and Sales groups)

This is a three-step pipeline: filter rows where salary exceeds 85, group surviving rows by department, then compute count and mean per group. The filter produces a bitmask; the group-by reads through it to build its index; the summarise collapses each group into one row.

Here’s a longer chain that shows more of the verb vocabulary:

let orders = Csv.parse("product,region,qty,price\nWidget,West,10,25.0\nGadget,East,5,40.0\nWidget,East,8,25.0\nGizmo,West,3,100.0\nWidget,West,12,25.0\nGadget,West,7,40.0\n");
let view = orders.view();

// Chain: filter → mutate → arrange → select
let report = view
    .filter(dexpr_binop("==", col("region"), "West"))   // keep West only
    .mutate("revenue", dexpr_binop("*", col("qty"), col("price")))  // add revenue column
    .view()                                               // back to immutable view
    .arrange([desc("revenue")])                           // sort by revenue descending
    .select(["product", "revenue"]);                      // keep only these columns

// Result: Gizmo/300.0, Widget/300.0, Widget/250.0, Gadget/280.0
// Original `orders` still has all 6 rows, all 4 columns, untouched

Each verb in the chain returns a new value — a TidyView, a TidyFrame, or a GroupedTidyView. The types guide what you can do next: you can’t call summarise unless you’ve called group_by first, and mutate returns a TidyFrame (because it creates new data) while filter and select return TidyView (because they only narrow what’s visible).

Grouped Aggregation with Multiple Statistics

let sales = Csv.parse("region,product,revenue\nWest,Widget,150\nEast,Gadget,200\nWest,Widget,300\nEast,Gadget,225\nWest,Gizmo,100\nEast,Widget,175\n");
let view = sales.view();

let summary = view
    .group_by(["region"])
    .summarise(
        "n",         tidy_count(),
        "total_rev", tidy_sum("revenue"),
        "avg_rev",   tidy_mean("revenue"),
        "min_rev",   tidy_min("revenue"),
        "max_rev",   tidy_max("revenue")
    );
// Groups appear in first-occurrence order: West first, then East
// West: n=3, total=550, avg=183.33, min=100, max=300
// East: n=3, total=600, avg=200.00, min=175, max=225

Group order is always first-occurrence — the group that appears first in the data appears first in the result. Not alphabetical, not hash-dependent. Deterministic.

Adding Computed Columns

let mutated = view.mutate("bonus", dexpr_binop("*", col("salary"), 10));
print(mutated.ncols());   // → 4 (dept, name, salary, bonus)

mutate returns a TidyFrame — a materialized copy with the new column appended. The original view still has three columns. This is the copy-on-write boundary: reads are free, writes trigger a copy.

Sorting and Lazy Permutation

let csv = "name,score\nAlice,85\nBob,92\nCharlie,78\nDiana,95\n";
let df = Csv.parse(csv);
let sorted = df.view().arrange([desc("score")]);
let top2 = sorted.slice_head(2);
print(top2.nrows());   // → 2 (Diana=95, Bob=92)

arrange computes a permutation vector but does not materialize a new DataFrame. The sorted order is stored lazily — operations like select, rename, and relocate pass the permutation through without resolving it. Only when you need physical data (e.g., materialize, filter, slice, group_by) does the sort actually reorder the rows. This means arrange followed by slice_head is significantly faster than sorting the entire dataset.

Joins

let left = Csv.parse("id,name\n10,Alice\n20,Bob\n30,Charlie\n").view();
let right = Csv.parse("id,dept\n10,Eng\n30,Sales\n40,HR\n").view();

let joined = left.inner_join(right, "id", "id");
print(joined.nrows());   // → 2 (id=10 and id=30 match)

Inner join keeps only rows where the key exists in both tables. Alice (id=10) matches Eng, Charlie (id=30) matches Sales. Bob (id=20) and HR (id=40) are dropped.

TidyView supports seven join types: inner_join, left_join, semi_join, anti_join, full_join, cross_join, and natural_join. All use BTreeMap-based lookups with borrowed keys — no per-row string allocation.

Reshaping

// pivot_longer: columns → rows
let wide = Csv.parse("name,math,english\nAlice,90,85\nBob,78,92\n");
let long = wide.view().pivot_longer(
    ["math", "english"],   // columns to pivot
    "subject",             // new name column
    "score"                // new value column
);
// Result: 4 rows (Alice/math/90, Alice/english/85, Bob/math/78, Bob/english/92)

// pivot_wider: rows → columns
let summary = long.view().pivot_wider("subject", "score");
// Back to: Alice/90/85, Bob/78/92

Distinct and Sampling

// distinct: unique rows by column subset
let unique_regions = view.distinct(["region"]);

// deterministic sampling: same seed → same rows, every time
let sample = view.slice_sample(100, seed: 42);

slice_sample uses a seeded LCG-based Fisher-Yates shuffle. Same seed, same 100 rows, every run. (Cross-platform determinism has been verified on Windows; Linux and macOS testing is ongoing.)

How to Audit a TidyView Pipeline

This is where the architecture pays off in practice. When something goes wrong in a data pipeline, the question is always: where did the data go wrong?

In TidyView, every intermediate step is inspectable because the original data is never modified. Every filter produces a bitmask you can examine. Every group_by produces a group index with key values and row indices. And the original data stays intact behind a shared reference, so you can always go back and check it.

Inspection Methods

At any point in a pipeline, you can call:

view.mask()           // → the bitmask — which rows are visible
view.proj()           // → the projection map — which columns are visible
grouped.group_index() // → group keys + row indices per group

From the mask:

  • mask.iter_set() gives the exact indices of visible rows
  • mask.count_ones() gives the count of surviving rows

From the projection:

  • proj.indices() gives which column indices are visible

The original data is always there behind the Rc<DataFrame> — no operation destroys it.

A Worked Example

Suppose you’re analyzing quarterly sales data and you get a result that looks wrong. In a traditional DataFrame library, you’d re-run the pipeline with print statements and hope to catch the problem. In TidyView, you can inspect every intermediate state because nothing was destroyed.

Here’s a concrete scenario. You have eight sales records and you want total revenue by product in the West region:

let sales = Csv.parse("product,region,revenue\nWidget,West,150\nGadget,West,200\nWidget,East,175\nGadget,East,225\nWidget,West,300\nGizmo,East,50\nGadget,West,180\nGizmo,East,75\n");
let view = sales.view();

let west = view.filter(dexpr_binop("==", col("region"), "West"));
let grouped = west.group_by(["product"]);
let summary = grouped.summarise("total_rev", tidy_sum("revenue"));

The summary shows Widget: 450, Gadget: 380. Is that right? Let’s audit backward.

Step 1: Which rows survived the filter?

The bitmask after filter(region == "West") has bits set at positions [0, 1, 4, 6]. That’s four rows out of eight. You can verify: row 0 is Widget/West/150, row 1 is Gadget/West/200, row 4 is Widget/West/300, row 6 is Gadget/West/180. All West. None of the East rows slipped through.

Step 2: How did the groups form?

The group index shows two groups in first-occurrence order:

  • Group “Widget” → base-frame rows [0, 4]
  • Group “Gadget” → base-frame rows [1, 6]

Notice: Gizmo isn’t here. Both Gizmo rows were East, so they were filtered out before grouping. If you expected Gizmo to appear, the bitmask tells you exactly why it didn’t — rows 5 and 7 have their bits cleared.

Step 3: Verify the aggregation

Widget revenue: row 0 (150) + row 4 (300) = 450. Gadget revenue: row 1 (200) + row 6 (180) = 380. Both use Kahan compensated summation, so even with millions of rows the result would be bit-identical across runs.

Step 4: Is the original data intact?

Yes. view.nrows() still returns 8. You can re-filter, re-group, or inspect any column at any time. If the final number is wrong, you trace backward through bitmasks and group indices until you find where your understanding diverged from reality.

This is fundamentally different from a system where df = df[df.region == "West"] discards the filtered-out rows. In that system, if the filter was wrong, the evidence is gone. In TidyView, the evidence is the bitmask, and the original data is still there to check against.

Catching Mistakes at Compile Time

TidyView’s type system and error reporting catch common mistakes before they become wrong numbers:

Typo in a column name:

view.filter(dexpr_binop(">", col("revnue"), 100));
// → Error: column `revnue` not found

Self-referencing mutate:

view.mutate("bonus", dexpr_binop("*", col("salary"), 0.1))
    .mutate("double_bonus", dexpr_binop("*", col("bonus"), 2));

The second mutate sees bonus because it’s a separate call on the result of the first. But if you tried to reference a column in the same mutate call that creates it:

view.mutate("a", dexpr_binop("+", col("x"), 1),
            "b", dexpr_binop("*", col("a"), 2));
// → Error: column `a` not found

This is snapshot semantics — all column references are validated against the column list frozen before any assignments. You can’t accidentally create an order-dependent pipeline within a single operation.

Type mismatch:

view.filter(dexpr_binop("+", col("name"), 1));
// → Error: type mismatch — expected Bool predicate, got Int

The filter predicate must evaluate to Bool. Arithmetic on a string column, or a predicate that returns a number instead of true/false, is caught immediately with an error that names the exact types involved.

Error Messages That Point to the Problem

When something goes wrong, TidyView’s error types name the exact issue:

ColumnNotFound("revnue")       // Typo — you meant "revenue"
DuplicateColumn("total")       // Two columns with the same name in select
PredicateNotBool { got: "Int" } // filter predicate returned Int, not Bool
TypeMismatch { expected: "Float", got: "Str" }  // arithmetic on a string column
LengthMismatch { expected: 1000, got: 500 }     // broadcast size mismatch
EmptyGroup                     // first() or last() called on a group with zero rows
CapacityExceeded { limit: 65535, got: 70000 }   // too many distinct levels for a factor column

Every error variant names the specific column, type, or value that caused the problem. You know what went wrong and where, not just that something failed.

EmptyGroup catches a subtle bug: if an aggressive filter removes all rows from a group and you then call first() or last() in your summarise, you get a clear error instead of a silent null or a crash. CapacityExceeded guards the factor column type — CJC-Lang’s factor columns use u16 codes (max 65,535 distinct levels) for cache efficiency, and this error tells you exactly when your data exceeds that budget.

Why Not Just Use Polars?

Polars is a good library. It’s fast, it has a lazy API, and it uses Arrow-backed columnar storage. If I were building a Python application, I’d probably use it.

But Polars makes different trade-offs than TidyView:

Polars TidyView
Grouping Hash-based (fast; order may vary by execution mode) BTreeMap (deterministic first-occurrence order)
Summation Optimized reduction (fast; may vary across platforms) Kahan compensated (deterministic, bit-identical)
Parallelism Rayon thread pool (high throughput) Single-threaded (deterministic, no scheduling variance)
Null model Nullable everywhere (Arrow validity bitmaps) Null-free by default, NullCol only where needed
Sampling System RNG Seeded LCG-based Fisher-Yates

None of Polars’ trade-offs are wrong — and Polars is significantly faster for most workloads. It’s a production-grade library optimized for throughput. TidyView is optimizing for a different property: reproducibility — the guarantee that the same pipeline on the same data always produces the same result, bit for bit, across repeated runs. (All benchmarks and determinism tests were run on Windows; cross-platform verification is ongoing.) TidyView will be slower than Polars on almost any benchmark. That’s the expected cost of determinism.

When your work requires an audit trail — when a regulator, a reviewer, or future-you needs to verify that a result is correct — that guarantee can matter more than raw speed.

Benchmark Results

The claims above — bitmask filtering is cheap, chained filters don’t copy data, determinism holds across runs — are testable. I wrote a 38-test benchmark suite that measures TidyView’s performance and verifies its architectural guarantees. Here are the results, run on my machine (Intel i7-11390H, Windows, Rust 1.91 release mode, 100K-row datasets).

A note on context. These benchmarks measure TidyView against itself — lazy vs. eager, scaling behavior, determinism proofs. They are not comparative benchmarks against Polars, DuckDB, or Pandas. TidyView is single-threaded and prioritizes determinism over throughput; it will be slower than production-optimized libraries on raw performance. The goal here is to verify that TidyView’s architectural claims hold, not to claim speed parity with tools that make different trade-offs.

A. Filter Microbenchmarks

Test Lazy (µs) Eager (µs) Ratio
Int filter, 100K rows 165 11,943 72×
Float filter, 100K rows 191 11,804 62×
String filter, 100K rows 352 6,255 18×
Int filter, 1M rows 2,002 157,135 79×

What this proves: Lazy bitmask filtering is 62–79× faster than eager materialization for numeric columns. The filter itself just scans one column and sets bits — no row copying, no allocation beyond the bitmask.

String filters benefit from a columnar fast path that evaluates the predicate directly against the column buffer (bitwise, not row-by-row) — bringing them to 352 µs, an 18× lazy/eager ratio.

The 1M-row int filter takes about 2 ms to produce a bitmask. Materializing — actually copying the surviving rows into a new DataFrame — takes 157 ms. If you’re chaining multiple filters, you save that materialization cost at every step.

B. Chained Filter Composition

Test Time (µs)
Single int filter, 100K 220
Two chained int filters, 100K 384
Int + string chain, 100K 609
Two string filters, 100K 813

What this proves: Chaining two filters costs roughly the sum of the individual filters, not more. The second filter reads through the first filter’s bitmask — it only examines rows where the bit is set. No intermediate DataFrame is created. The cost of the chain is dominated by the most expensive predicate type (string comparison).

C. Group-By + Summarise

Test Groups Time (µs)
Count, 4 groups, 100K 4 3,099
Mixed agg (5 stats), 4 groups, 100K 4 5,918
Count + Sum, 100 groups, 100K 100 10,734
Count, 10K groups, 100K 10,000 42,778
Count + Sum + Mean, 10K groups, 100K 10,000 39,782

What this proves: Group-by cost scales with the number of distinct groups, not just row count. With 4 groups (the region column), grouping 100K rows takes about 3 ms. With 10K groups, it takes 40 ms. This is the cost of the BTreeMap-based index — O(N log G) where G is the number of groups. Adding more aggregation functions adds relatively little overhead; the dominant cost is building the group index, not computing the aggregates.

The single-key fast path (used when grouping by one column) avoids per-row heap allocation entirely: the BTreeMap key is a borrowed reference into the column data, not a cloned value. For the 4-group case, this means 100K lookups with zero string copies.

D. Sorting

Test Time (µs)
Float descending, 100K 1,378
Int ascending, 100K 1,201
String ascending, 100K 18,343
Multi-key (string + float), 100K 41,638

What this proves: Numeric sorts on 100K rows complete in about 1.2–1.4 ms thanks to pre-resolved column references — the sort comparator holds a direct &Column pointer instead of doing a name lookup on every comparison. String sorting is roughly 13× more expensive than numeric sorting due to comparison cost. Multi-key sorting adds the cost of a secondary comparator.

All sorts use Rust’s stable sort (sort_by), which preserves the relative order of equal elements — important for determinism. The result is stored as a lazy permutation vector, not a new DataFrame. Operations like select, rename, and relocate propagate the permutation without resolving it.

E. Joins

Test Time (µs)
Inner join, 100K × 100 33,936
Inner join, 100K × 1K 40,507
Left join, 100K × 100 38,712

What this proves: Join performance is dominated by the left table size. Increasing the right table from 100 to 1,000 rows adds about 20% to the join time, because the right table is indexed and lookups are O(log K) where K is the number of unique keys.

Both the right-side index build and the left-side probe use borrowed keys — ColumnKeyRef references directly into the column data without cloning. For single-key joins (the common case), the BTreeMap key is a single borrowed value with no Vec wrapper, eliminating per-row allocation on both sides.

F. Full Pipeline Benchmarks

Pipeline Time (µs)
filter → group → summarise → arrange, 100K 4,151
filter → mutate → group → summarise, 100K 40,640
join → filter → group → summarise, 100K 97,769

What this proves: Realistic multi-step pipelines compose predictably. The filter-group-summarise-arrange pipeline (4.2 ms) is faster than the sum of its parts because the filter reduces the number of rows that downstream operations process, and the lazy sort avoids materializing a new DataFrame. The join-based pipeline is slowest because joins are the most expensive operation and produce a new materialized DataFrame.

G. Scaling Behavior

Row scaling (filter):

Rows Filter Time (µs)
10K 17
100K 149
1M 2,345

Filter time scales linearly with row count — exactly what you’d expect from a single-pass scan. 10× more rows ≈ 10× more time.

Row scaling (group-by + summarise):

Rows Time (µs)
10K 384
100K 4,065
1M 65,106

Group-by also scales linearly, but with a higher constant factor due to the BTreeMap insertions. The single-key fast path keeps this manageable: 1M rows grouped in 65 ms.

Column width scaling (100K rows):

Columns Filter (µs) Materialize (µs)
3 183 957
11 153 2,520
51 187 20,388
101 247 36,450

What this proves: Filter time is nearly constant regardless of table width — because filtering only touches the predicate column, not the others. Materialization scales linearly with column count because it has to copy every column. This is the columnar storage payoff: a table with 101 columns filters as fast as a table with 3 columns.

Group cardinality scaling (100K rows):

Groups Time (µs)
4 7,914
100 17,814
1,000 29,940
10,000 42,220
50,000 58,239

Going from 4 groups to 50,000 groups increases time by about 7.4×. The BTreeMap index is O(N log G), so the cost grows sub-linearly with group count.

H. Determinism Verification

Test Result
Pipeline (sum + mean + sd + median), 10 runs Bit-identical
Sort stability (equal keys preserve insertion order) Stable
Group order (first-occurrence across 5 runs) Deterministic

What this proves: The determinism guarantee isn’t aspirational — it’s measured. Ten runs of the same pipeline produce identical floating-point results down to the last bit. The sort is stable (equal elements preserve their original order), and groups always appear in first-occurrence order. Additionally, five proptest property suites (200 random cases each) verify these invariants hold across random inputs.

I. Memory Budget

Test Result
1M-row bitmask 122 KB (vs 15,625 KB data = 128× smaller)
3 chained filters, 100K rows Each mask: 1,563 words. Rows: 74,999 → 49,999 → 24,999

What this proves: A bitmask for 1 million rows costs 122 KB — one bit per row, packed into 64-bit words. The data itself (just the id and value columns) occupies 15.6 MB. The bitmask is 128× smaller than the data it describes. Three chained filters create three separate bitmasks, each 1,563 words (12.5 KB), while the underlying data is shared. The row counts shrink progressively (75K → 50K → 25K) as each filter narrows the result, but no data is copied until you explicitly materialize.

J. Trust and Audit Trail

These are not performance benchmarks — they’re correctness proofs that verify TidyView’s architectural claims.

Test What it verifies
Filter traceability After two filters on a 10-row dataset, the bitmask contains exactly [0, 4, 8]. You can name every surviving row by index.
Group membership Groups appear in first-occurrence order (Eng, Sales, HR — not alphabetical). Each group’s row indices are inspectable: Eng = [0, 2], Sales = [1, 4], HR = [3, 5].
Snapshot semantics mutate("a", ..., "b", col("a") * 2) fails with column 'a' not found — you can’t reference a column created in the same mutate call.
Copy-on-write isolation Clone a frame, mutate the clone — the original has 2 columns, the clone has 3. They’re independent.
Full pipeline trace Filter → group → summarise on 8 rows: west_rows = [0, 1, 4, 6], Widget total = 450, Gadget total = 380. Original still has 8 rows.
Deterministic sampling slice_sample(100, seed=42) selects the same 100 rows across 5 runs.
Projection inspection select(["b", "d"]) on a 4-column table sets projection indices to [1, 3]. Original table still reports 4 columns.

What this proves: Every architectural claim made earlier in this post — bitmask traceability, group ordering, snapshot semantics, copy-on-write isolation, deterministic sampling — is verified by a concrete test with concrete numbers.

The One-Sentence Summary

TidyView is a database engine wearing a tidyverse costume, designed so that same data + same code = same result on every run, every time — verified on Windows, with cross-platform testing still ahead.

It combines familiar dplyr verbs (filter, select, mutate, group_by, summarise) with database-engine internals (columnar storage, bitmask filtering, predicate pushdown, zone maps) to deliver data pipelines that are deterministic by construction, auditable by design, and memory-safe without runtime surprises.

CJC-Lang is open source and available on crates.io. The TidyView implementation lives in crates/cjc-data/ — about 14,000 lines of Rust with zero external dependencies. The full benchmark suite (38 tests, 10 categories) is in tests/tidyview_benchmarks.rs.

item.title: Chess RL in CJC-Lang: Training a Chess Agent from Scratch with Zero Dependencies
item.dc:creator: Adam Ezzat
item.description:
🕑 ~20 min read

I built a chess-playing AI from scratch — in a programming language I also built from scratch. No PyTorch. No NumPy. No Stockfish. Zero external dependencies.

This post is a technical walkthrough of how that works: the language design decisions that made it possible, the RL algorithm, the chess engine, the autodiff engine, the performance engineering, and what I learned along the way.

The Live Demo

Before diving into the technical details, here’s the training visualization running live. This shows the agent playing self-play chess games, with real-time stats from the v2.5 training run:

Tip

The board animates through sample self-play games. The stats, speed bars, and evolution list are all real numbers from the v2.5 training run.

What is CJC-Lang?

CJC-Lang (Computational Jacobian Core) is a numerical programming language I wrote in Rust — 21 crates, roughly 96,000 lines of code. It’s designed for ML pipelines, numerical systems, and scientific computing where reproducibility matters.

Reproducibility is a first-class concern, not an afterthought. The language enforces it through specific engineering choices:

  • Kahan summation for all floating-point reductions — prevents accumulation error from corrupting results
  • Ordered data structures throughout (BTreeMap/BTreeSet, never HashMap) — no iteration-order non-determinism
  • Explicit seed threading via SplitMix64 — every source of randomness is traceable
  • No FMA instructions in SIMD paths — fused multiply-add can produce different results on different hardware
  • Two execution backends (AST interpreter + MIR register machine) that are required to produce identical results

The result: same input, same output. Every time.

CJC-Lang has built-in tensors, automatic differentiation, copy-on-write memory, pattern matching, closures, structs, for/while loops, if-expressions, file I/O, and SVG visualization. The compiler pipeline looks like this:

flowchart LR
    S["Source\n.cjcl"] --> L["Lexer"] --> P["Parser"] --> A["AST"] --> T["Type\nChecker"] --> H["HIR"] --> M["MIR"] --> E["Executor"]

CJC-Lang compiler pipeline. Source code flows through seven stages before execution.

The chess RL demo exercises nearly every one of these language features — it’s the most demanding program CJC-Lang runs.

The Chess Engine

The chess engine is written entirely in CJC-Lang. Not a wrapper around Stockfish. Not a simplified variant. A complete implementation.

Move Generation

Legal move generation covers every piece type with every special rule:

  • Pawns: single push, double push from starting rank, diagonal capture, en passant capture, promotion to queen/rook/bishop/knight
  • Knights, bishops, rooks, queen: full movement patterns with board-boundary checking
  • King: standard moves plus castling (kingside and queenside, with path-clear and through-check validation)

Board Representation

The board is encoded as a 774-dimensional input vector for the neural network:

12 piece planes x 64 squares = 768 features
+ 4 castling rights (K, Q, k, q)
+ 1 normalized move clock (for 50-move rule)
+ 1 en passant file indicator
= 774 total input features

Each piece plane is a binary 64-element vector indicating where that piece type exists on the board. This gives the network a spatially structured view of the position.

Board encoding in CJC-Lang 
fn encode_board(board: Any, color: i64, castling: Any,
                move_clock: i64, ep_file: i64) -> Tensor {
    let features = [];
    let piece_types = ["P","N","B","R","Q","K","p","n","b","r","q","k"];
    let p = 0;
    while p < 12 {
        let sq = 0;
        while sq < 64 {
            if board[sq] == piece_types[p] {
                features = array_push(features, 1.0);
            } else {
                features = array_push(features, 0.0);
            }
            sq = sq + 1;
        }
        p = p + 1;
    }
    // Castling rights
    features = array_push(features, float(castling[0]));
    features = array_push(features, float(castling[1]));
    features = array_push(features, float(castling[2]));
    features = array_push(features, float(castling[3]));
    // Normalized move clock
    features = array_push(features, float(move_clock) / 100.0);
    // En passant
    features = array_push(features, float(ep_file) / 8.0);
    Tensor.from_vec(features, [1, 774])
}

This is the CJC-Lang version. In production, encode_state_fast (a native builtin) replaces this with an O(774) buffer fill instead of O(38 x 774) COW array copies — same output, 30x fewer allocations.

Draw Detection

The engine handles three draw conditions:

  1. 50-move rule — a counter tracks half-moves since the last pawn push or capture
  2. Insufficient material — detects K vs K, K+B vs K, K+N vs K
  3. Threefold repetition — this one’s interesting

Threefold repetition detection uses Zobrist hashing: a 64-bit hash for each board position, computed by XOR-ing random numbers assigned to each (piece, square) combination. The hash updates incrementally with each move (XOR out the old position, XOR in the new one), making it O(1) per move instead of O(64) for a full board comparison.

fn zobrist_hash(board: Any, color: i64, castling: Any) -> i64 {
    let h = 0;
    let sq = 0;
    while sq < 64 {
        if board[sq] != "" {
            let piece_idx = piece_to_index(board[sq]);
            h = bitxor(h, ZOBRIST_TABLE[sq * 12 + piece_idx]);
        }
        sq = sq + 1;
    }
    if color == 1 { h = bitxor(h, ZOBRIST_SIDE); }
    // ... castling rights hashed similarly
    h
}

The position history stores these hashes, and a repetition is detected by counting occurrences — no need to store or compare full board states.

The RL Algorithm

The demo uses A2C (Advantage Actor-Critic) with GAE (Generalized Advantage Estimation). This is a real, modern RL algorithm — the same family used in many production systems.

Network Architecture

flowchart LR
    B["Board\nEncoding\n[1, 774]"] --> H1["Hidden 1\n774 → 128\nReLU"] --> H2["Hidden 2\n128 → 64\nReLU"]
    H2 --> PF["Policy: From\n64 → 64\nSoftmax"]
    H2 --> PT["Policy: To\n64 → 64\nSoftmax"]
    H2 --> V["Value Head\n64 → 1\ntanh"]

A2C network architecture. The 774-D board encoding flows through shared hidden layers, then splits into policy and value heads.

The network has 45,873 trainable parameters across these layers:

Layer Shape Parameters
Hidden 1 (weights + bias) 774 x 128 + 128 99,200
Hidden 2 (weights + bias) 128 x 64 + 64 8,256
Policy from-head (weights + bias) 64 x 64 + 64 4,160
Policy to-head (weights + bias) 64 x 64 + 64 4,160
Value head (weights + bias) 64 x 1 + 1 65
Total ~45,873

The policy uses factored action heads: instead of outputting probabilities over all possible moves (which would be thousands), it predicts a from-square distribution and a to-square distribution independently. The score for a move is the sum of the from-square log-probability and the to-square log-probability. This factorization keeps the output layer small (128 outputs instead of 4,096+).

Advantage Estimation (GAE)

GAE computes advantages by looking at multi-step returns with exponential weighting:

where is the TD error.

The hyperparameters: (discount factor), (GAE smoothing). These control the bias-variance tradeoff in advantage estimation — higher gives lower bias but higher variance.

Training Loop

Each training episode:

  1. Rollout: play a full game via self-play, recording states, actions, log-probabilities, rewards, and value estimates at each step
  2. Compute returns and advantages: walk backward through the trajectory using GAE
  3. Policy loss: — nudge the policy toward moves with positive advantage
  4. Value loss: — improve value function predictions
  5. Backward pass: compute gradients through the entire network via autodiff
  6. Adam update: update all 45,873 parameters
A2C update step in CJC-Lang 
fn a2c_update_adam(params: Any, trajectory: Any, config: Any,
                   adam_state: Any, step: i64) -> Any {
    let advantages = compute_gae(trajectory, config);
    advantages = whiten(advantages);

    let policy_loss = 0.0;
    let value_loss = 0.0;
    let t = 0;
    while t < len(trajectory) {
        policy_loss = policy_loss - trajectory[t].log_prob * advantages[t];
        let val_err = trajectory[t].value - trajectory[t].return_;
        value_loss = value_loss + val_err * val_err;
        t = t + 1;
    }
    policy_loss = policy_loss / float(len(trajectory));
    value_loss = value_loss / float(len(trajectory));

    let total_loss = policy_loss + 0.5 * value_loss;

    // Backward pass through autodiff
    grad_graph.zero_grad();
    grad_graph.backward(total_loss);

    // Adam optimizer update for each parameter tensor
    let p = 0;
    while p < len(params) {
        let grad = grad_graph.grad(params[p].idx);
        params[p] = adam_step(params[p], grad, adam_state[p], step,
                              config.lr, 0.9, 0.999, 1e-8);
        p = p + 1;
    }
    params
}

Additional training features:

  • Temperature annealing (1.2 → 0.8): starts with high exploration, gradually becomes more greedy
  • Advantage whitening: normalizes advantages to zero mean and unit variance for stable gradients
  • Resignation threshold: the agent can resign when its value estimate drops below a threshold, saving compute on hopeless games
  • Move-count penalty: small penalty per ply (0.001) to discourage aimless play

The Autodiff Engine

This is where CJC-Lang’s runtime does the heavy lifting. The RL algorithm above calls grad_graph.backward(loss) — but what happens inside that call?

GradGraph: Arena-Based Automatic Differentiation

CJC-Lang’s autodiff engine is a reverse-mode automatic differentiation system. It builds a directed acyclic graph (DAG) of operations during the forward pass, then traverses it backward to compute gradients.

The original implementation (v2.0) used the standard Rust approach: Vec<Rc<RefCell<GradNode>>> — each node was a reference-counted, interior-mutable object. This works, but every .borrow() call has runtime overhead, and each node allocation hits the heap individually.

In v2.4, the engine was redesigned to use arena allocation: flat arrays for operations, tensors, and gradients. This eliminated roughly 120 .borrow() calls per backward pass.

v2.0: Each node is a separate heap allocation
  [Node 0] → [Node 1] → [Node 2] → ...  (Rc<RefCell<GradNode>> per node)

v2.4+: Flat arena arrays — one contiguous block
  ops:     [Op0, Op1, Op2, Op3, ...]      (Vec<GradOp>)
  tensors: [T0,  T1,  T2,  T3,  ...]      (Vec<Tensor>)
  grads:   [G0,  G1,  G2,  G3,  ...]      (Vec<Option<Tensor>>)

Dead Node Elimination

Not every node in the computation graph is reachable from the loss. In a multi-head architecture (policy + value), the backward pass from the policy loss doesn’t need gradients for nodes only used by the value head (and vice versa during the value backward pass).

Before traversing backward, the engine builds a reachability set from the loss node. Unreachable nodes are skipped entirely — typically 20-30% of the graph for multi-head networks.

In-Place Gradient Accumulation (v2.5)

The original gradient accumulation created a new tensor every time two gradients were added:

// Before: allocates a new tensor
grads[idx] = Some(existing.add_unchecked(&incoming));

In v2.5, this was replaced with in-place mutation:

// After: mutates existing tensor
existing.add_assign_unchecked(&incoming);

This uses Buffer::borrow_data_mut() to get a mutable reference to the underlying data and update it element-wise. The result: roughly half the tensor allocations per backward pass are eliminated. This single change was responsible for the v2.4 → v2.5 speedup (43.5 → 29.8 seconds per episode).

Fused MLP Layers (v2.5)

A standard dense layer in the computation graph creates four separate nodes:

  1. Transpose the weight matrix
  2. Matrix multiply input by transposed weights
  3. Add bias
  4. Apply activation function

The fused GradOp::MlpLayer collapses all four into a single graph node:

MlpLayer {
    input: usize,    // index of input tensor
    weight: usize,   // index of weight tensor
    bias: usize,     // index of bias tensor
    activation: Activation,  // ReLU, Tanh, etc.
}

This reduces graph nodes by 3x per layer and computes all three parameter gradients (d_input, d_weight, d_bias) in a single fused backward pass.

Performance Engineering

The Profiling-Driven Approach

Every optimization was driven by measurement, not intuition. CJC-Lang has built-in profiling builtins (profile_zone_start, profile_zone_stop, profile_dump) that I used to instrument the training loop.

The first profile revealed the bottleneck distribution:

Function % of Rollout Time
score_moves (forward pass per move) 84.2%
legal_moves (move generation) 7.7%
apply_move (board update) 0.1%
Everything else 8.0%

84% of time was spent evaluating the neural network for each legal move. The fix: score_moves_batch, a native Rust builtin that evaluates all legal moves in a single batched forward pass instead of looping through them one at a time in the interpreter.

Result: 7.7x rollout speedup.

The Amdahl’s Law Moment

Here’s where it got interesting. A 7.7x speedup on the rollout phase should have been transformative. But total episode time barely moved:

Before: rollout ~10.4s + backward ~25s = ~35s
After:  rollout ~1.3s  + backward ~25s = ~26s
Speedup: ~1.35x (not 7.7x)

Amdahl’s Law in action. The rollout was only about 30% of total episode time. Making it 7.7x faster saved ~9 seconds, but the backward pass (which was untouched) still took 25 seconds. The bottleneck had shifted.

This insight redirected the entire optimization strategy. Instead of making the interpreter faster, I redesigned the autodiff engine:

Version Change Episode Time Speedup
v2.3 Native rollout kernels 82.2 s baseline
v2.4 Arena GradGraph + dead node elimination 43.5 s 1.89x
v2.5 In-place gradients + fused MLP 29.8 s 2.76x

Native Hot-Path Kernels

Three performance-critical functions were promoted from CJC-Lang code to native Rust builtins:

adam_step — Element-wise Adam optimizer update. The CJC-Lang version was a scalar loop over every parameter; the native version operates directly on tensor buffers. ~9x faster.

encode_state_fast — Board-to-tensor encoding. The CJC-Lang version used array_push to build the 774-element feature vector, which triggered a copy-on-write clone at each push. The native version fills a pre-allocated buffer in a single pass. O(n) vs O(38n).

score_moves_batch — Batched move scoring. Instead of calling the forward pass once per legal move (in the interpreter), this evaluates all moves in a single native call with one matrix multiplication. 7.7x rollout speedup.

All three builtins produce bit-identical output to their CJC-Lang counterparts — verified by parity tests.

Training Results

Honest Numbers

After 120 episodes of self-play training:

  • Elo change: +13.9 — the first measurable learning signal in the v2.x series
  • Win rate vs random: 50% — not yet reliably beating a random opponent
  • Win rate vs greedy: 50% — ties with a material-greedy baseline
  • Non-zero terminal rewards: 6/120 episodes — most games are still drawn

These are modest results. The agent is not strong. I report this honestly because the goal was never to compete with Stockfish or AlphaZero — it was to demonstrate that a from-scratch language can express, train, and evaluate a real RL agent.

What This Proves

The demo exercises nearly every CJC-Lang feature simultaneously:

Language Feature How It’s Used
Tensors Board encoding, weight matrices, gradients
Autodiff (GradGraph) Full A2C loss backward through policy + value
Closures Evaluation callbacks, training hooks
Pattern matching Piece type dispatch, move classification
Structs Board state, game config, training stats
For/while loops Rollout iteration, move generation
If-expressions Legal move filtering, terminal detection
Arrays (COW) Move lists, trajectory buffers
Deterministic RNG SplitMix64 seed threading
File I/O CSV training logs, PGN game records
Vizor (SVG) Training curve visualization

It’s a single CJC-Lang program (~1,715 lines) that implements the chess engine, RL algorithm, optimizer, evaluation suite, Elo computation, PGN notation, checkpoint system, and training curve plotting.

The v1 to v2.5 Journey

For context, here’s how far the demo has come:

Dimension v1 (March 2026) v2.5 (April 2026)
Network 1,072 params 45,873 params (43x)
Input 66 features 774 features (12x)
Gradients 95 lines of hand-rolled chain rule Full automatic differentiation
Optimizer Manual SGD Adam (native builtin)
Chess rules Basic (no castling) Complete
Algorithm REINFORCE A2C + GAE
Training speed N/A 29.8 s/episode
Learning signal None Elo +13.9
Determinism Bit-identical Still bit-identical

The Stack

CJC-Lang program (~1,715 LOC)
    Chess engine + A2C + GAE + Adam + Elo + PGN + checkpoints
        |
CJC-Lang compiler + runtime (Rust, ~96K LOC)
    Lexer → Parser → AST → Type Checker → HIR → MIR → Executor
    Autodiff: arena-based GradGraph with fused MLP ops
    451 builtins, all deterministic
    21 crates, zero external dependencies

Every tensor operation, every gradient computation, every optimizer step runs in code I own. No framework sits between the algorithm and the math.

Same seed = same game. Always.

What’s Next

The agent’s Elo +13.9 is a starting point, not a destination. The infrastructure is now solid — the bottleneck is no longer the language or the autodiff engine, it’s the ML itself. Larger networks, longer training, curriculum learning, and MCTS are all expressible in CJC-Lang as it stands today.

The deeper goal isn’t chess strength. It’s showing that a carefully designed language — one that takes reproducibility seriously, owns its entire stack, and grows to meet the demands of its hardest workload — can do real work.

If you want to try CJC-Lang yourself: cargo install cjc-lang, then cjcl run your_program.cjcl.

CJC-Lang is open source. The compiler, runtime, and all demos are available on GitHub.

item.title: Syntax Guide for CJC Version 0.1.1
item.dc:creator: Adam Ezzat
item.description:
🕑 ~45 min read
21Rust crates
287+builtins
6,500+tests passing
What’s New in v0.1.1

CJC-Lang v0.1.1 adds Quantum Hardening Phase 3 (Jordan-Wigner, Suzuki-Trotter, Zero-Noise Extrapolation), AST Evolution v0.3 (visitor trait, metrics, validation), MIR Evolution v0.3 (schedule metadata, reduction analysis, SSA loop overlay), and MPS enhancements (canonical forms, SWAP networks). This guide covers every language feature and builtin available as of this release.

Part 1 — The Language

CJC-Lang is a statically-aware, dynamically-typed language for deterministic scientific computing. If you’ve written Rust, Python, or R, the syntax will feel familiar. This section covers every construct the language supports.

Functions

Every function parameter requires a type annotation. Return types are optional but recommended.

fn add(a: i64, b: i64) -> i64 {
    a + b
}

fn greet(name: String) {
    print("Hello, " + name);
}

Functions are first-class values. You can pass them as arguments:

fn apply(f: fn(i64) -> i64, x: i64) -> i64 {
    f(x)
}

fn double(x: i64) -> i64 { x * 2 }

fn main() -> i64 {
    apply(double, 21)
}

Recursive functions work as expected:

fn factorial(n: i64) -> i64 {
    if n <= 1 { return 1; }
    n * factorial(n - 1)
}

fn main() -> i64 { factorial(10) }

Generic functions use angle-bracket syntax with optional trait bounds:

fn identity<T>(x: T) -> T { x }

fn my_max<T>(a: T, b: T) -> T {
    if a > b { a } else { b }
}

fn wrap<T>(x: T) -> T { x }
fn apply_generic<T>(x: T) -> T { wrap(x) }

Variables

Immutable by default. Use let mut for mutation.

let x: i64 = 42
let pi = 3.14159
let name = "CJC-Lang"

let mut count = 0
count = count + 1

CJC-Lang supports type inference, but you can always be explicit:

let a: f64 = 1.0
let b: bool = true
let c: String = "hello"
let d: i64 = 99

Primitive Types

Type Description Example
i64 64-bit signed integer 42, -7
f64 64-bit floating point 3.14, 1e-6
bool Boolean true, false
String UTF-8 string "hello"
ByteSlice Raw byte sequence b"hello"
Any Dynamic/polymorphic type Used for generic dispatch

Control Flow

if/else

if works as both a statement and an expression:

// Statement form
if x > 0 {
    print("positive");
} else {
    print("non-positive");
}

// Expression form — both branches must return compatible types
let abs_x = if x >= 0 { x } else { -x };

Note: no parentheses around the condition. Braces are always required.

while loops

let mut sum = 0;
let mut i = 1;
while i <= 100 {
    sum = sum + i;
    i = i + 1;
}

for loops

for iterates over ranges using start..end (exclusive upper bound):

let mut total = 0;
for i in 1..11 {
    total = total + i;
}

break and continue

Both work inside while and for loops:

for i in 0..10 {
    if i == 4 { break; }
    print(i);
}

let mut i = 0;
while i < 5 {
    i = i + 1;
    if i == 3 { continue; }
    print(i);
}

Pattern Matching

CJC-Lang has full structural pattern matching with match expressions:

// Match on integers
let x = 99;
let label = match x {
    1 => "one",
    2 => "two",
    _ => "other",
};

// Match on enums
enum Color { Red, Green, Blue }

fn classify(c: Color) -> i64 {
    match c {
        Red   => 1,
        Green => 2,
        _     => 3,
    }
}

Pattern matching supports destructuring:

// Option type
let x = Some(42);
let val = match x {
    Some(v) => v,
    None    => 0,
};

// Result type
let r = Ok(3.14);
match r {
    Ok(v)  => print(v),
    Err(e) => print("error"),
}

Structs

struct Point { x: f64, y: f64 }

let p = Point { x: 1.5, y: 2.5 };
print(p.x + p.y);

Structs can have methods via impl blocks:

struct Circle { radius: f64 }

impl Circle {
    fn area(c: Circle) -> f64 {
        3.14159 * c.radius * c.radius
    }
}

let c = Circle { radius: 5.0 };
print(Circle::area(c));

Generic structs:

struct Pair<T, U> { first: T, second: U }

Enums

enum Shape {
    Circle(f64),
    Rectangle(f64, f64),
    Triangle(f64, f64, f64),
}

enum Option<T> { None, Some(T) }

Traits

trait Numeric: Add + Mul {
    fn zero() -> Self;
    fn one() -> Self;
}

impl<T> Vec<T> : Iterable {
    fn len(self: Vec<T>) -> i64 { 0 }
}

Closures

Closures use |args| body syntax with mandatory type annotations on parameters:

let f = |x: f64| x * x;
let add = |x: f64, y: f64| x + y;

print(f(3.0));
print(add(1.0, 2.0));

Closures capture variables from their enclosing scope. CJC performs capture analysis during HIR lowering to determine what each closure needs.

Arrays

Arrays are ordered, dynamically-typed collections:

let nums = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5];
let mixed = [1, "hello", 3.14, true];

// Array operations — note: array_push returns a NEW array
let extended = array_push(nums, 6);
let length = array_len(extended);
let first = array_get(nums, 0);
let sliced = array_slice(nums, 1, 3);

array_push(arr, val) does not mutate arr. It returns a new array. Always reassign: arr = array_push(arr, val);

Strings

let s = "hello world";
let upper = str_upper(s);
let lower = str_lower(s);
let trimmed = str_trim("  padded  ");
let parts = str_split("a,b,c", ",");
let joined = str_join(["a", "b", "c"], "-");
let sub = str_contains(s, "world");

Formatted strings use f"..." syntax:

let name = "CJC-Lang";
let version = "0.1.1";
print(f"Welcome to {name} v{version}");

Byte Slices

For low-level data processing:

let data: ByteSlice = b"name,age,score\nAlice,30,95.5";
let rows = data.split_byte(b'\n');
let header = rows[0];
let fields = header.split_byte(b',');

Compound Assignments

CJC recognizes +=, -=, *=, /= operators:

let mut x = 10;
x += 5;
x -= 3;
x *= 2;
x /= 4;

Pipe Operator

The |> operator chains function calls left-to-right:

data |> filter(predicate) |> map(transform)

nogc Blocks

Mark functions as allocation-free for performance-critical paths. The NoGC verifier statically checks that no GC-triggering operations occur inside:

nogc fn fast_multiply(x: f64, y: f64) -> f64 {
    x * y
}

The MIR pipeline includes a NoGC verification pass that performs call-graph analysis with a may_gc fixpoint computation. If any reachable function allocates, the verifier rejects the program.

Part 2 — The Compiler Pipeline

CJC compiles through a multi-stage pipeline. Understanding it helps you reason about error messages, optimization, and the dual-engine execution model.

Source → Lexer → Parser → AST → [TypeChecker] → HIR → MIR → [Optimize] → Exec

Stage 1: Lexer

The lexer (cjc-lexer) converts source text into a flat token stream. It recognizes all CJC-Lang keywords, operators (including |>, +=, @), literals (integers, floats, strings, byte strings), and identifiers.

let (tokens, diags) = Lexer::new(src).tokenize();

Stage 2: Parser

A Pratt parser (cjc-parser) builds the AST from tokens. It handles operator precedence, expression vs. statement disambiguation, and produces structured error diagnostics.

let (program, diags) = cjc_parser::parse_source(src);

The parser recognizes 35 expression kinds, 9 statement kinds, 11 declaration kinds, and 9 pattern kinds.

Stage 3: AST

The AST (cjc-ast) is a tree of typed nodes. As of v0.1.1, the AST layer includes:

  • Visitor trait (visit module) — read-only traversal covering all node variants with walk_* functions
  • Metrics (metrics module) — single-pass structural statistics: node counts, depth, operator frequencies, feature flags
  • Validation (validate module) — structural checks: break/continue outside loops, duplicate params, empty match, unreachable code after return, nesting depth limits
  • Inspect (inspect module) — deterministic text dumps: dump_ast_summary, dump_ast_metrics, dump_validation_report, dump_expr_tree
  • Node utilities (node_utils module) — helper methods like Expr::child_count, is_literal, Block::is_empty, Program::has_main_function

Stage 4: HIR (High-level IR)

HIR lowering (cjc-hir) desugars the AST. for loops become while loops over ranges. Closures get capture analysis. The HIR is closer to the execution model while still being readable.

Stage 5: MIR (Mid-level IR)

MIR (cjc-mir) is a register-based intermediate representation. HIR lowers to MIR instructions that operate on virtual registers.

The MIR layer in v0.1.1 includes:

  • Optimizer — constant folding, strength reduction, dead code elimination (DCE), common subexpression elimination (CSE), loop-invariant code motion (LICM)
  • SSA construction — Cytron’s minimal SSA algorithm with 6 optimization passes
  • Loop analysis — enriched LoopInfo with is_countable, trip_count_hint, num_exits, schedule metadata
  • Reduction analysis — enriched ReductionInfo with accumulator_semantics (Plain, Kahan, Binned, RuntimeDefined)
  • Schedule metadata — descriptive execution hints (SequentialStrict, DescriptiveTiled, DescriptiveVectorized, etc.) that are non-semantic
  • SSA loop overlay — maps SSA definitions to loops, identifies loop-carried variables, cross-references reductions with SSA accumulators
  • Legality verifier — schedule consistency checks, reduction metadata cross-validation
  • NoGC verifier — call-graph may_gc fixpoint analysis

Stage 6: Execution (Dual Engine)

CJC-Lang has two execution engines that must produce identical results:

Engine Module Entry Point Description
v1 cjc-eval Interpreter::new(seed).exec(&program) AST tree-walk interpreter
v2 cjc-mir-exec run_program_with_executor(&program, seed) MIR register-machine executor

The MIR executor also supports an optimized mode:

cjcl run program.cjcl --mir-opt

Parity tests in the test suite verify that both engines produce bit-identical output for every supported program.

Diagnostics

CJC-Lang uses structured error codes (E0xxx-E8xxx) via the cjc-diag crate. Diagnostics carry source spans, severity levels, and optional fix suggestions.

Part 3 — Determinism Architecture

Determinism is CJC-Lang’s defining architectural constraint. Every design decision flows from one requirement: same seed in, same bits out, every time, on every platform.

How CJC Achieves Determinism

Component Mechanism
RNG SplitMix64 with explicit seed threading — no global state
Collections BTreeMap/BTreeSet everywhere — never HashMap/HashSet
Floating-point sums Kahan summation or BinnedAccumulator — never naive reduction
SIMD No FMA (fused multiply-add) — mul_fixed throughout
Complex arithmetic mul_fixed for all complex multiplications
Iteration Vec-based storage with deterministic ordering
Parallelism Results identical regardless of thread count

Kahan Summation

Naive floating-point addition loses precision as terms accumulate. CJC-Lang uses compensated summation everywhere:

// Under the hood, CJC-Lang's runtime uses Kahan summation:
// sum = 0.0, compensation = 0.0
// for each term:
//   y = term - compensation
//   t = sum + y
//   compensation = (t - sum) - y
//   sum = t

This matters for scientific workloads where you’re summing millions of terms. The result is the same whether you sum them on a laptop or a cluster.

BinnedAccumulator

For even higher-precision requirements, CJC-Lang provides binned accumulators that partition the floating-point range into bins, accumulate within each bin, then combine — guaranteeing reproducible results even under reordering.

SplitMix64

All randomness in CJC-Lang flows through a seeded SplitMix64 generator. There is no global RNG state. Every function that needs randomness takes an explicit seed parameter:

let samples = q_sample(circuit, 1000, 42);
let measurement = q_measure(circuit, 42);

Part 4 — Builtin Functions

CJC-Lang ships with 287+ builtin functions registered in cjc-runtime. These are available without imports in every CJC-Lang program.

Mathematics

Function Description
abs(x) Absolute value
sqrt(x) Square root
pow(base, exp) Exponentiation
log(x) Natural logarithm
log2(x), log10(x) Base-2 and base-10 logarithm

sin(x), cos(x), tan(x) Trigonometric functions
asin(x), acos(x), atan(x) Inverse trigonometric
sinh(x), cosh(x), tanh(x) Hyperbolic functions

min(a, b), max(a, b) Minimum and maximum
clamp(x, lo, hi) Clamp to range
gcd(a, b), lcm(a, b) Greatest common divisor, least common multiple
factorial(n) Factorial
fibonacci(n) Fibonacci number

Statistics

Function Description
mean(arr) Arithmetic mean
median(arr) Median
variance(arr) Sample variance
std_dev(arr) Standard deviation
percentile(arr, p) p-th percentile
quantile(arr, q) Quantile
correlation(x, y) Pearson correlation
covariance(x, y) Covariance
skewness(arr) Skewness
kurtosis(arr) Kurtosis
zscore(arr) Z-score normalization
mann_whitney(x, y) Mann-Whitney U test
percent_rank(arr) Percent rank
ntile(arr, n) N-tile grouping
case_when(conds, vals, default) Conditional value selection

Arrays

Function Description
array_len(arr) Length
array_push(arr, val) Append (returns new array)
array_pop(arr) Remove last element (returns new array)
array_get(arr, idx) Get element at index
array_set(arr, idx, val) Set element (returns new array)
array_slice(arr, start, end) Subsequence
array_contains(arr, val) Membership test
array_reverse(arr) Reverse
array_sort(arr) Sort (deterministic)
array_map(arr, f) Map function over elements
array_filter(arr, f) Filter by predicate
array_reduce(arr, f, init) Left fold
array_flatten(arr) Flatten nested arrays
array_zip(a, b) Zip two arrays

Strings

Function Description
str_len(s) Length
str_upper(s), str_lower(s) Case conversion
str_trim(s) Strip whitespace
str_split(s, delim) Split by delimiter
str_join(arr, delim) Join array with delimiter

Tensor Operations

Function Description
Tensor.from_vec(data, shape) Create tensor from flat data + shape
t.shape() Get shape
t.sum() Sum all elements
t.mean() Mean of all elements
t.transpose() Transpose
t.matmul(other) Matrix multiplication
